Dollar surges after Trump speech dashes Iran ceasefire hopes

The U.S. dollar surged on Thursday, clawing back two days of losses after President Donald Trump's primetime address on the Iran war dashed hopes of an imminent ceasefire and sent investors scrambling for safe-haven assets. Gold and silver fell sharply as the stronger greenback and rising Treasury yields undercut precious metals, while the British pound slid on renewed fears over the UK's exposure to the Middle East energy crisis.

Published
4 minutes ago
mitrade
US Dollar Index advances to near 100.00 as Trump sets no clear ...
aljazeera
Trump's primetime speech on Iran war: Key takeaways
apnews
Read the complete transcript of Trump's address to the nation
cnbc
Dollar gains as Trump sets no clear Iran ceasefire timeline in speech
cnbc.com
Trump Speech Reverses Market Optimism

The dollar index climbed back toward the 100 level in early European trading on Thursday, after Trump's Wednesday night address offered no clear timeline for ending the conflict. Speaking from the White House, Trump said his "core objectives are nearing completion" but indicated the U.S. military would continue strikes on Iran for another two to three weeks. He threatened to bring Iran "back to the Stone Ages" and target the country's electric grid if no agreement is reached.
aljazeera
+3

The speech upended two days of cautious optimism. Earlier in the week, the dollar had weakened after reports that Trump was willing to end the war and Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian expressed Tehran's "necessary will" to negotiate. The euro had climbed to one-week highs above 1.16 on Wednesday before reversing course, and the Australian dollar also gave back gains.
mexc
+2

7 sources
Gold and Silver Retreat

Gold fell from multi-day highs, retreating from around $4,720 per ounce recorded on Wednesday morning. The combination of a firmer dollar and elevated Treasury yields — which have risen sharply since the conflict began in late February — made the non-yielding metal less attractive. Silver, which carries higher volatility than gold, dropped more steeply as speculative positions unwound.
fortune
+2

The precious metals selloff mirrored a familiar pattern during the conflict: when peace hopes rise, gold and silver gain on a weaker dollar, only to reverse when escalation resumes. Traders are now pricing in nearly a 52% probability of a Federal Reserve rate increase by the end of 2026, according to CNBC, the first time that threshold has been crossed.
mitrade

4 sources
Sterling Under Pressure

The pound was among the hardest-hit major currencies, sliding from Wednesday's close of around $1.33 as traders priced in the implications of prolonged conflict for the UK economy. Britain faces outsize risk from the energy crisis due to its heavy reliance on gas — around 30% of its electricity comes from gas-fired plants compared with 17% in Germany and just 3% in France, according to Reuters.
poundsterlinglive
+1

The International Monetary Fund warned this week that the UK is "especially exposed" to the energy shock, comparing the impact to "a large sudden tax on income" for households. The OECD has separately predicted that UK growth will be more adversely affected than any other G20 nation. Oil prices have risen roughly $40 a barrel since the conflict began, costing UK consumers an estimated £22 billion annually, or about 0.7% of national income, according to the Financial Times.
news.yahoo
+1

Anthropic issues 8,000 takedowns after Claude Code leak

Anthropic inadvertently published the entire source code for Claude Code, its popular AI-powered coding assistant, after an employee included a debug file in a routine software update on Tuesday. The company is now racing to scrub copies of the code from the internet, issuing thousands of copyright takedown notices on GitHub as the leaked files continue to spread.

Published
18 hours ago
fortune
Anthropic mistakenly leaks its own AI coding tool's source ... - Fortune
bloomberg
Anthropic Accidentally Exposes System Behind Claude Code
thehackernews
Claude Code Source Leaked via npm Packaging Error, Anthropic Confirms
news.bloomberglaw
Anthropic Accidentally Posts Source Code of Claude AI Agent (1)
pcmag.com
The Leak

Security researcher Chaofan Shou first flagged the exposure on X, writing that "Claude code source code has been leaked via a map file in their npm registry". The post quickly went viral, amassing tens of millions of views. The leak originated from version 2.1.88 of the Claude Code npm package, which contained a 60-megabyte source map file that pointed to a zip archive hosted on Anthropic's Cloudflare R2 storage bucket. That archive held roughly 1,900 TypeScript files comprising more than 512,000 lines of proprietary code.
thehackernews
+4

Anthropic confirmed the incident in a statement: "No sensitive customer data or credentials were involved or exposed. This was a release packaging issue caused by human error, not a security breach. We're rolling out measures to prevent this from happening again".
fortune
+2

The exposed code pertains to the "agentic harness" that wraps around Anthropic's underlying AI model — the software layer that instructs Claude how to use tools, enforces behavioral guardrails, and manages developer interactions — rather than the model weights or training data themselves.
ndtv
+1

8 sources
Containment Efforts

By Wednesday morning, Anthropic had filed copyright takedown requests that led to the removal of more than 8,000 repositories on GitHub, according to The Wall Street Journal. The company later acknowledged the sweep was overly broad and narrowed its request to 96 repositories. But developers had already begun converting the TypeScript codebase into other programming languages using AI tools in an effort to sidestep copyright claims.
wsj
+2

3 sources
A Pattern of Lapses

The incident marks Anthropic's second security stumble in a matter of days. Just last week, Fortune reported that the company had left nearly 3,000 unpublished files publicly accessible, including a draft blog post describing a powerful forthcoming model known internally as "Mythos" or "Capybara". A similar source map exposure also affected an earlier version of Claude Code in February 2025.
ndtv
+2

The repeated lapses have drawn scrutiny toward a company that has built its brand around AI safety and responsible development. As Bloomberg reported, the leak raises questions about Anthropic's operational security at a time when Claude Code has become a key revenue driver for the startup.
bloomberg
+1

Google faced similar backlash earlier this month when users of its Antigravity coding tool protested pricing changes. Anthropic, which does not publish exact token limits for its subscription tiers, instead describes them in...

OpenAI rated the vulnerability as critical and has remediated the issue across all affected interfaces following responsible disclosure, according to Decipher. Fixes reportedly include input validation, stronger shell escaping, and reduced token scope and lifetime. The selloff echoes a similar episode in February, when Anthropic's launch of Claude Code Security — a tool that autonomously scans code for vulnerabilities — triggered a multi-day rout across the sector. Nvidia says 40% of revenue now comes from non-cloud buyers

Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang's absence from India's AI Impact Summit in February has drawn renewed attention as the chipmaker's staggering order pipeline and broadening revenue base underscore its dominance in the global AI economy.

Published
19 hours ago
bloomberg
Nvidia Makes Trillion-Dollar Forecast at Annual Product Expo

Nvidia Expects At Least $1T In AI Chip Orders by 2027 - Entrepreneur

Nvidia locks $1 trillion in Blackwell, Vera Rubin orders | The Tech Buzz

Nvidia GTC 2026: CEO Jensen Huang keynote Blackwell Vera Rubin

A Trillion-Dollar Pipeline

At Nvidia's GTC 2026 developer conference on March 16, Huang told a packed audience that the company expects $1 trillion in orders for its Blackwell and next-generation Vera Rubin chip architectures through 2027. The figure doubled the company's prior estimate of $500 billion, which Huang had projected just months earlier. Bloomberg reported that Huang unveiled a range of new products during a two-and-a-half-hour keynote, including plans to push into central processing units and chips for orbital data centers.
bloomberg
+3

The order book spans hyperscalers including Amazon Web Services, Microsoft, Google, and Meta, as well as a growing roster of enterprise and sovereign AI customers. In fiscal 2026, Nvidia posted full-year revenue of $215.9 billion, up 65% from the prior year, with data center revenue alone reaching $197.3 billion.
letsdatascience
+2

7 sources
Revenue Diversification Takes Shape

Huang has pointed to the company's expanding customer base beyond the largest cloud providers. Throughout fiscal 2026, major cloud service providers accounted for roughly 50% of data center revenue each quarter, meaning the other half came from enterprises, sovereign AI programs, and infrastructure partnerships. Countries including Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Taiwan, and Sweden are building national AI platforms on Nvidia hardware, and sovereign AI revenue tripled to over $30 billion in fiscal 2026.
finance.yahoo
+1

Why Huang Missed New Delhi

Huang had been expected to be one of the marquee attendees at the India AI Impact Summit, held at Bharat Mandapam in New Delhi from February 16 to 20. Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi inaugurated the event. But Reuters reported on February 14 that Nvidia confirmed Huang would not attend, citing "unforeseen circumstances". Nvidia's managing director for South Asia, Vishal Dhupar, later said Huang was unwell after three consecutive weeks of extensive travel, and that senior executive Jay Puri led the company's delegation instead. India's IT Minister Ashwini Vaishnaw said during a briefing that the decision was a personal one.
economictimes
+2

Despite Huang's absence, the summit drew other prominent figures, including Google CEO Sundar Pichai and OpenAI CEO Sam Altman.

Nvidia reported fiscal year 2026 revenue of $215.9 billion and guided for approximately $78 billion in...

As Mashable noted, the common thread in Huang's public statements is "a recurring tendency to define the criteria in a manner that makes 'yes, we're there' the most convenient response". By his own admission, the kind of intelligence needed to replicate what Nvidia itself has accomplished remains out of reach — raising the question of...

Turkey, Australia brace for prolonged economic pain from Iran war

Turkey's finance minister sought to reassure investors this week that the economic fallout from the Iran war remains under control, even as Australia's prime minister warned his nation of months of hardship ahead — underscoring how the conflict that began on February 28 is rippling through economies worldwide.

Published
10 hours ago
dailysabah
Economic effects of Iran war manageable for Türkiye: Şimşek | Daily Sabah
reuters
POLL Turkey's central bank to pause rate cuts as U.S.-Iran war weighs
turkiyetoday
Oil shock manageable, Türkiye sticks to economic course: Finance ...
+27 sources
yahoo.com
Turkey Calls Impact 'Manageable'

Treasury and Finance Minister Mehmet Şimşek told investors in London that the short-term economic effects of the war are "negative but manageable," citing "limited warflation, wider current account deficit, slower growth" in a presentation shared during the meetings. Despite rising risks, Şimşek said there has been no change in the government's macroeconomic priorities and that disinflation remains its top goal. Annual inflation in Turkey stood at 31.5% in February, down from 75% in 2024, but expectations have risen amid soaring energy prices.
dailysabah
+1

If oil prices average around $85 per barrel in 2026, inflation could rise by roughly 3.6 to 4.4 percentage points and economic growth could fall by 0.6 to 1.5 percentage points, according to Şimşek's presentation. Turkey's central bank halted its easing cycle in March, holding the benchmark rate at 37% after five consecutive cuts, while effectively tightening policy by shifting overnight funding to around 40%. Central bank governor Fatih Karahan said the war has driven "a pronounced increase in energy prices, exerting cost-push pressure on inflation," adding that the bank is "determined to ensure the tightness required for the continuation of the disinflation process".
aa
+3

5 sources
Australia Braces for Months of Pain

Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese delivered a rare televised address on Wednesday, cautioning that the economic shocks from the conflict "will be with us for months". "Australia is not directly involved in this conflict. However, all Australians are experiencing higher costs as a result," he said. The government announced temporary measures including halving fuel excise taxes and eliminating road user fees for heavy vehicles for three months, while establishing a National Fuel Security Plan. Bloomberg reported that Albanese is also expected to announce A$1 billion in interest-free loans for businesses hit by soaring energy costs.
bbc
+2

3 sources
Global Ripple Effects

The war's disruption of roughly 20% of global oil supply through the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz has sent oil prices surging more than 40% from pre-war levels, according to The New York Times and Fox Business. Brent crude has traded above $100 per barrel in recent weeks, with analysts warning prices could climb further. Goldman Sachs projected Brent could average $115 per barrel in April under its baseline scenario.
foxbusiness
+4

Central banks across the world have responded by shelving plans for rate cuts. The European Central Bank, Bank of England, Bank of Japan, and the U.S. Federal Reserve all held rates steady in March, with major brokerages now forecasting possible rate hikes from the ECB and Bank of England as early as April, according to Reuters. As Şimşek noted in his London remarks, the conflict may also reshape supply chains and create new trade corridors — but for now, the immediate economic burden is what governments are scrambling to contain.
thestreet
+2

8 sources
Discover more
Iran war batters Asian economies with record currency lows and energy crises
The disruption to naphtha — a petroleum derivative and essential feedstock for plastics — is threatening South Korean industries from food packaging to automobiles to medical supplies. About 45 percent of the world's naphtha passes through the Strait of Hormuz, which Iran's military declared effectively closed on March 27. Naphtha prices have more than doubled since January, surging from $595...
ADB, S&P warn Iran war threatens global inflation surge
The IMF has cautioned that if crude oil remains above $100 per barrel for a year or longer, global inflation could rise by up to two percentage points and output could decline by about one percent. With Brent crude having climbed more than 40% since the conflict began, the question facing policymakers everywhere is not whether the war will raise prices, but for how long.

Renfe Suspends High-Speed Train Project to Paris Due to Certification Roadblocks

Renfe has decided to indefinitely suspend its high-speed rail project between Spain and Paris after nearly a decade of frustrated attempts to certify its trains in France. The Spanish state-owned operator has terminated the Framework Agreement it had with SNCF Réseau to operate on the high-speed corridor to the French capital, as exclusively reported by eldiario.es.
eldiario

The decision stems from what Renfe sources describe as "accumulated difficulties in the certification process in France and the impossibility of establishing a reliable timeline for its completion." The company has withdrawn its reserved operating track slots on the high-speed corridor between Paris and Lyon and has terminated the certification process for the Avril train, the S-106 model manufactured by Talgo, with which it intended to operate the service.

+1

A Decade of Obstacles

Renfe signed a contract in 2017 to acquire around thirty high-speed trains, ten of which were prepared to operate in both Spain and France. Since then, the approval process has been riddled with obstacles. The connection was supposed to be operational in 2024, coinciding with the Paris Olympic Games, but administrative and technical delays prevented this. In early 2025, Renfe's Director General of Global Strategy, Paloma Baena, acknowledged before the French Senate that the approval process would not be completed before the end of 2028.
rtve
+1

The Spanish operator and the Minister of Transport, Óscar Puente, have repeatedly denounced the lack of reciprocity with France: while SNCF operates Ouigo in Spain with relative ease, Renfe has run into a regulatory wall in the neighboring country. The company points out that France maintains "a high level of its own regulations, highly complex in nature" and limited deployment of the European ERTMS signaling system, which hinders interoperability.
lavanguardia
+1

4 sources
This Isn't a Final Goodbye

Renfe emphasizes that the suspension does not mean giving up on operating in France. The company will maintain its connections from Barcelona to Lyon and Marseille, which have been active since 2023 and have served more than 640,000 passengers. "We remain committed to resuming this work when technical and operational conditions allow," company sources indicated.
eldiario

Additionally, Renfe is pursuing an alternative route to Paris: the operator is preparing a bid for the public tender for the Paris-Dijon-Lyon regional line, a contract valued at approximately 1 billion euros over ten years, with the initial offer due before April 13, according to Forbes España. The award is expected in the second half of 2027, with operations planned to begin between 2029 and 2030.

New AI model diagnoses five brain diseases from one blood test

Researchers at Lund University in Sweden have developed an artificial intelligence model that can detect five different neurodegenerative conditions from a single blood sample, a advance that could reshape how doctors diagnose dementia-related diseases. The study, published March 30 in Nature Medicine, describes a system called ProtAIDe-Dx that simultaneously identifies Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, ALS, frontotemporal dementia, and previous stroke.
lunduniversity.lu
+1

Published
22 hours ago
lunduniversity.lu
New AI model can detect multiple cognitive brain diseases from a ...
neurosciencenews
AI Identifies Multiple Dementias from One Blood Sample
sflorg
New AI model can detect multiple cognitive brain diseases from a ...
nature
A deep joint-learning proteomics model for diagnosis of six ... - Nature
sciencenews.org
How It Works

The AI model was trained on protein measurements from more than 17,000 patients and control participants, drawn from the Global Neurodegenerative Proteomics Consortium's database — the largest in the world for proteins related to neurodegenerative diseases. Using a technique called "joint learning," the model identifies a shared set of proteins that form a general pattern for brain degeneration, then uses that pattern to distinguish between individual diseases.
neurosciencenews
+1

"Our hope is to be able to accurately diagnose several diseases at once with a single blood test in the future," said Jacob Vogel, an assistant professor at Lund University who led the study.
lunduniversity.lu

The approach addresses a persistent challenge in neurology: different neurodegenerative conditions often present with overlapping symptoms, making it difficult to distinguish between them, particularly in early stages of cognitive decline.
neurosciencenews
+1

2 sources
Better Than Clinical Diagnosis Alone

The researchers found that the protein profile generated by the model predicted cognitive decline more accurately than clinical diagnosis alone. It also revealed that patients with the same clinical diagnosis may have different underlying biological subtypes.
sflorg
+1

"It seems like individuals with the same clinical diagnosis may have different underlying biological subtypes," said Lijun An, the study's first author and a postdoctoral researcher at Lund University. Many individuals diagnosed with Alzheimer's disease showed protein patterns more similar to other brain disorders, suggesting they may have more than one underlying condition or that Alzheimer's can develop in multiple ways.
lunduniversity.lu
+1

2 sources
Limitations and Next Steps

Vogel cautioned that blood-based protein measurements alone will not be sufficient for multi-disease diagnosis. "We need to refine the method and combine it with other clinical diagnostic tools," he said. The team plans to incorporate additional proteomic markers using mass spectrometry to identify patterns unique to each disease.
lunduniversity.lu

The study was validated across multiple independent datasets, a distinction from earlier research in the field. It was supported by Sweden's SciLifeLab, the Wallenberg Data Driven Life Science Program, and the U.S. National Institutes of Health, among other funders.
neurosciencenews

A key finding was that nearly 70 percent of HCC cases in the UK Biobank arose in patients without prior diagnoses of chronic liver disease such as cirrhosis or viral hepatitis — a gap that current screening guidelines, which focus primarily on patients with known cirrhosis, fail to address. To make the tool practical for everyday clinical use, the team ran an ablation experiment that distilled...

JPMorgan Asset snaps up Treasuries and gilts after war-driven selloff

JPMorgan Asset Management is snapping up government bonds in the US and UK, seeing value in shorter-dated debt that has been battered by aggressive bets on higher interest rates following the Iran war. The move comes as bond markets begin to shift focus from inflation fears to concerns about economic growth.

Published
45 minutes ago
jpmorgan
Review of markets over the first quarter of 2026 | J.P. Morgan Asset ...

JPMorgan Asset Buys Treasuries, Gilts Dented by Inflation Risks

Finding Value in the Front End

Arjun Vij, a Hong Kong-based money manager at JPMorgan Asset Management, is targeting the two-to-five-year part of the yield curve, identifying opportunities in the US, Australia, and the UK, according to a report published Thursday. Short-dated bonds were among the hardest-hit assets during the first quarter, as surging oil prices from the Iran conflict forced markets to rapidly reprice expectations for central bank policy from rate cuts to rate hikes.
jpmorgan
+2

Government bond markets endured a punishing selloff in March. In the UK, the two-year gilt yield jumped from 3.5% to a peak of 4.6%, while the 10-year yield rose from 4.2% to above 5%, according to Euronews. In the US, the 10-year Treasury yield climbed as much as 48 basis points from pre-war levels, according to Business Insider. The selloff reflected what BCA Research's chief fixed income strategist Robert Timper called "aggressive bear flattening" driven by "hawkish monetary policy repricing in response to inflation fears".

A Turning Tide in Rate Expectations

However, the mood has begun to shift. Bond traders have started abandoning inflation bets as elevated oil prices increasingly threaten growth rather than just prices, Bloomberg reported. Early last week, futures had priced in a rate hike by year-end as a near certainty; by early this week, rate swaps reflected only about 25% odds of cuts by the end of 2026.
bloomberg

The bank now expects just one rate hike in 2026, in June, down from a previous forecast of two increases in April and July.

Carney says he spoke with Trump about Middle East war

Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney said on Wednesday that he spoke with U.S. President Donald Trump about developments in the rapidly escalating Middle East conflict, according to Reuters. The call came on a day of intensifying diplomatic activity, as the war between the U.S., Israel, and Iran — now in its 32nd day — continues to roil global energy markets and reshape alliances.

Diplomacy on Two Tracks

The exchange follows a similar call between the two leaders on March 9, when Carney posted on X that they had discussed "the economy, developments in the Middle East, and trade relations between our two countries". Canada initially backed the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran that began February 28 but has since called for a diplomatic off-ramp, with Carney telling an audience in Sydney in early March that the strikes appeared "inconsistent with international law".

Global consumers shift to defensive spending as Iran war drags on

A month into the U.S.-Israel military campaign against Iran, consumers across the globe are pulling back on spending, hoarding cash, and bracing for higher prices as the conflict drives up energy costs and clouds the economic outlook. From Dubai's luxury malls to British supermarkets and Australian petrol stations, a defensive shift in household behavior is taking hold.

UAE Households Retreat to Essentials

In the United Arab Emirates, about 65% of households are now spending only on essentials or actively stockpiling, according to strategy consulting firm Redseer Middle East. "Discretionary spending is sharply down," managing director Sandeep Ganediwalla told the Economic Times, noting that spending on electronics, furniture, and clothing has seen 35 to 38% of consumers reduce or stop purchases entirely. Dubai, a global luxury retail hub, has been hit hardest: roughly 70% of luxury buyers have curtailed purchases, with nearly a quarter postponing them indefinitely.
economictimes.indiatimes

The UAE government announced a fuel price hike effective April 1 to reflect surging global crude prices, a move likely to deepen the pullback. Authorities have also issued 216 fines against traders and retailers caught raising prices amid a surge in demand for staple goods, part of a broader crackdown following more than 8,000 inspection tours, according to The National.

In Australia, Commonwealth Bank's Household Spending Insights Index fell 0.5% in February, the first decline in 17 months, with transport and utilities among the hardest-hit categories. "Energy costs don't just affect petrol — they flow through to food, freight and manufactured goods," she said. CBA now expects headline inflation to rise to around 5.4% by mid-2026 under its central scenario, with lower household spending expected to be the main drag on economic growth.

In the United Kingdom, a KPMG Consumer Pulse survey conducted in early March found that half of Britons who believed the economy was weakening had cut spending as a result, with 40% deferring major purchases — up from 34% the previous quarter. Grocery and energy costs tied to the conflict were the leading concerns. The GfK Consumer Confidence Barometer dropped to minus 21 in March, its lowest since April 2025, with a plunge in willingness to make large purchases, the BBC reported.

Meanwhile, the Institute of Grocery Distribution warned that UK food inflation could more than double from its current rate of 3.6% to briefly exceed 8% by June under a severe energy shock scenario, adding over £150 to the average household's annual grocery bill. The OECD has suggested the UK faces the steepest growth hit among G20 nations from the conflict. As GfK's Neil Bellamy put it: "There is a wave of anxiety spreading. Analysts say US can't replace jet fuel lost to Hormuz blockade

Energy analysts swiftly dismissed President Donald Trump's suggestion that countries struggling to secure jet fuel amid Iran's blockade of the Strait of Hormuz should simply buy from the United States, pointing to data showing American exports fall far short of the volumes lost from the critical waterway.

Trump wrote on Truth Social on Tuesday that countries unable to obtain jet fuel "because of the Strait of Hormuz" should "buy from the U.S., we have plenty," while also urging them to "build up some delayed courage, go to the Strait, and just TAKE IT".

The Numbers Don't Add Up

Data from vessel-tracking firm Kpler shows that roughly 500,000 barrels per day of jet fuel normally flow out of the Strait of Hormuz, primarily to Europe but also to Asia and Africa. By contrast, the U.S. Energy Information Administration reports that American jet fuel exports averaged just 219,000 barrels per day last year — less than half the disrupted volume.

"It's very, very unlikely the U.S. will be able to replace Strait of Hormuz supplies," Matt Smith, a Kpler analyst, told Reuters. "Even if the U.S. has plenty of jet fuel, there are also plenty of airlines."

Seizing Iran's uranium by force would risk casualties, experts warn

Should the United States attempt to seize Iran's stockpile of highly enriched uranium by military force, the operation would require as many as 1,000 specially trained troops, carry radiation and chemical hazards, and likely result in casualties, according to experts and former government officials.
military

The warning, detailed in an Associated Press report from Vienna, underscores the tension between President Donald Trump's stated goal that Iran will "never have a nuclear weapon" and the practical difficulties of achieving it on the ground.
military

Published

Securing Iran's Enriched Uranium by Force Would Be Risky and ...

U.S.-Iran nuclear talks wrap up with no announcement of a deal as ...

Iran's Stockpile of Highly Enriched Uranium: Worth Bargaining For?

U.S. Negotiators Were Ill-Prepared for Serious Nuclear Negotiations ...
euronews.com
A Stockpile Shrouded in Uncertainty

Iran possesses 440.9 kilograms of uranium enriched to 60% purity — a short technical step from the 90% weapons-grade threshold — enough for as many as 10 nuclear bombs, according to the International Atomic Energy Agency. IAEA inspectors have not been able to verify the location of the material since June 2025, when joint Israeli and American strikes targeted Iran's nuclear infrastructure.
nprillinois
+2

IAEA Director General Rafael Grossi has said roughly 200 kilograms is believed to be stored in tunnels at a nuclear complex outside Isfahan, with additional quantities at the Natanz and Fordo facilities. U.S. Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard told a House hearing on March 19 that the intelligence community has "high confidence" it knows the stockpile's location.
military

3 sources
Hazmat Suits and Booby Traps

The uranium is stored as hexafluoride gas in canisters weighing about 50 kilograms each. David Albright, a former nuclear weapons inspector and founder of the Institute for Science and International Security, warned that damaged canisters could release fluorine, a highly toxic chemical, and that spacing them improperly during transport risks triggering a critical nuclear reaction.
military

Christine Wormuth, former Army secretary under President Joe Biden and now head of the Nuclear Threat Initiative, called it a "very complex and high risk military operation" that would "probably take casualties." She said the Isfahan site alone could require 1,000 personnel, heavy equipment flown in by helicopter, and possibly a temporary airstrip. Special forces would need to work alongside nuclear experts in tunnels potentially filled with rubble, decoys, and booby traps.
military

"The Iranians have thought this through, I'm sure, and are going to try to make it as difficult as possible," Wormuth said.
military

1 source
The Case for Diplomacy

Experts emphasized that a negotiated settlement remains the safest path. Scott Roecker, a former director at the National Nuclear Security Administration and now a vice president at the Nuclear Threat Initiative, pointed to the 1994 "Project Sapphire" operation, in which the U.S. and Kazakhstan cooperated to secretly remove 600 kilograms of weapons-grade uranium left over from the Soviet program.
military

Grossi said on March 22 that the IAEA is "considering these options, of course," but added: "Nothing can happen while bombs are falling." U.S.-Iran nuclear talks in Geneva in late February ended without a deal, and Trump recently signaled the war could wind down within weeks — leaving the fate of the uranium stockpile as a central unresolved question.

12,000-year-old dice found in North America rewrite gambling's origins

Long before the Bronze Age civilizations of Mesopotamia began carving six-sided cubes, Native Americans were fashioning two-sided dice from small pieces of bone and using them in games of chance at the close of the last Ice Age, according to new research published in the journal American Antiquity.

A team led by Robert Madden, a researcher at Colorado State University, has identified what appear to be the oldest known dice in the world — artifacts dating back more than 12,000 years to the Late Pleistocene period, found at archaeological sites across the western Great Plains. The finding pushes back the origins of dice by more than 6,000 years beyond what was previously recognized. Until now, the earliest known dice were thought to have emerged roughly 5,500 years ago during the Bronze Age in Mesopotamia and the Indus Valley.
Daily Mail
+1

Published
dailymail.co
Caveman casino! Humans began gambling 12,000 YEARS ago, scientists say – as they discover ancient dice in the western Great Plains
wsj
Humans Have Been Playing With Dice for Longer Than We Thought
nationalgeographic
Prehistoric Dice Boards Found—Oldest Games in Americas?
+79 sources
sciencenews.org
A New Way of Seeing Old Artifacts

Using a novel classification method, the researchers re-examined artifacts that had long been cataloged as potential "gaming pieces" or had been overlooked entirely. The analysis identified nearly 600 likely dice from 57 archaeological sites spanning a 12-state area and covering every major period of North American prehistory. The earliest examples date from approximately 12,000 to 10,200 years ago.
wsj
+1

"Western scholars have treated dice and probability as Old World innovations," Madden said, according to the Wall Street Journal. He argued that the archaeological evidence reveals ancient Native American societies were intentionally creating objects designed to produce random outcomes and incorporating them into structured games thousands of years earlier than previously acknowledged.
Daily Mail
+1

2 sources
Games as Social Fabric

The dice described in the study are simple, two-sided pieces crafted from bone — humble objects, but ones that point to a deep tradition of social interaction through gaming. "They're straightforward, elegant tools," Madden said.
Daily Mail

The findings build on decades of research into the role of games in Indigenous cultures. Barbara Voorhies, a research professor of anthropology at the University of California, Santa Barbara, has long argued that gambling is deeply embedded in human prehistory. "It shows up everywhere, from the arctic to the tropical lowlands, and in hunter-gatherer to more complex societies," Voorhies has said. Earlier work by Voorhies had identified roughly 5,000-year-old dice-game scoreboards at the Tlacuachero site in Mexico's Chiapas state, previously considered among the oldest gaming evidence in the Americas.
nationalgeographic
+2

4 sources
Rewriting the Timeline

The study's scope underscores the enduring role of dice games across Native American cultures, from Ice Age hunter-gatherers to later complex societies. Rather than a peripheral curiosity, the research suggests that games of chance were a persistent feature of social life on the continent for millennia — a tradition that predates writing, agriculture, and the great civilizations of the ancient Near East.

A Message Over the Heads of Washington

Pezeshkian's letter followed an earlier message posted on his X account on Monday, March 30, in which he referenced the mass "No Kings" protests across all 50 U.S. states, claiming that "the American people are angry about 'Israel First'" and are "tired of Israeli kings ruling over American democracy". Iran's Parliament Speaker Mohammad Baqer Qalibaf echoed the sentiment, telling American protesters: "Welcome to the party we started 47 years ago — no kings".

Dueling Signals Amid Conflict

The letter arrived on the same day as several other developments. Reuters reported that Vice President JD Vance had spoken to Pakistani intermediaries about the Iran conflict as recently as Tuesday, while President Trump told reporters he expected U.S. military operations in Iran to conclude within two to three weeks, according to The New York Times. The White House confirmed Trump would address the nation about the conflict on Wednesday evening.
reuters

Iran, for its part, has rejected any suggestion it is seeking a ceasefire, with its government calling Trump's claims to that effect "false and baseless". Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said Tehran is prepared for "at least six months" of war with the United States and Israel.

+1

Broader Context

Pezeshkian has walked a complicated line throughout the conflict, at times signaling willingness to negotiate and at others vowing defiance. In early February, he ordered the start of nuclear talks with Washington, and in a March 7 televised address, he apologized to Gulf neighbors for Iranian retaliatory strikes while insisting Iran would "never surrender". A classified U.S. intelligence assessment reported by The Washington Post concluded that even a large-scale military offensive would be unlikely to topple Iran's government.
anews

The open letter's tone — addressed not to the White House but to the American public — recalls a tactic used by Iran's late Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who wrote an open letter to American university students in 2024 during the Gaza protests.

Evercore ISI reiterated an Outperform rating and $260 price target, while TD Cowen maintained its Buy rating with a $250 target, calling Slackbot "a key enabling technology for Salesforce to generate cross-domain execution of AI agents". Mizuho reaffirmed an Outperform rating at $265, JMP Securities held its Market Outperform rating with a $315 target, and BTIG maintained a Buy rating with a $255 target. All targets sit well above the stock's recent trading level near $187, down roughly 30% year-to-date.

Hormuz crisis and EU nuclear push intensify pressure on Spain's reactor phaseout

The closure of the Strait of Hormuz amid the ongoing war involving the United States, Israel, and Iran has thrown Europe's energy security into sharp relief, accelerating a continent-wide embrace of nuclear power that is leaving Spain increasingly isolated in its commitment to shutting down all seven of its reactors by 2035.

Published
13 hours ago
standupfornuclear
Spain Page - Stand Up for Nuclear
strategicenergy
The ENTSO-E report issued today does not blame renewables for ...
entsoe
ENTSO-E Publishes Expert Panel Final Report on 28 April 2025 ...
usnews
Iberian Blackout Was Caused by Multiple Factors, Report Says
ans.org
A Blackout Revisited

On March 20, ENTSO-E published its final expert panel report on the April 28, 2025, blackout that left large parts of Spain and Portugal without power for up to 16 hours — the first event of its kind in more than two decades. The investigation concluded that the blackout "resulted from a combination of many interacting factors, including oscillations, gaps in voltage and reactive power control, differences in voltage regulation practices, rapid output reductions and generator disconnections in Spain, and uneven stabilisation capabilities". The findings offered partial vindication for Spain's Socialist government, which had faced accusations that over-reliance on renewable energy caused the collapse. The report did not single out renewables, instead pointing to failures across conventional and renewable generators alike, as well as outdated manual voltage control systems and non-compliant protection settings.
strategicenergy
+3

Yet the political fallout continues. Audio recordings played before Spain's Senate on March 30 captured grid operators linking the crisis to renewables in real time, with one Red Eléctrica technician exclaiming moments before the system collapsed. Spain's conservative People's Party has seized on such evidence to demand the government reverse the 2019 phaseout agreement, under which the first reactor at Almaraz is set to close in 2027-28.
techxplore
+2

7 sources
A Nuclear Pivot Across Europe

The energy shock from the Middle East has given the nuclear lobby powerful new ammunition. Iran's retaliatory strikes on March 18 damaged an estimated 30 to 40 percent of Gulf oil refining capacity, and European gas prices have surged roughly 70 percent since the conflict began. On March 31, EU Energy Commissioner Dan Jorgensen warned governments to prepare for "prolonged disruption" to energy markets.
euronews
+3

Against that backdrop, European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen used the Nuclear Energy Summit in Paris on March 10 to declare that turning against nuclear power had been "a strategic mistake". She announced a €200 million guarantee, funded through the EU Emissions Trading System, to support private investment in innovative nuclear technologies, including small modular reactors the bloc aims to have operational by the early 2030s. Four more countries — Belgium, Brazil, China, and Italy — endorsed the Declaration to Triple Nuclear Energy by 2050 at the summit, bringing the total to 38 signatories, a list that also includes Sweden, Poland, and France.
world-nuclear-news
+3

8 sources
Spain's Narrowing Options

Spain now finds itself increasingly out of step with its neighbors. Its seven reactors still supply around one-fifth of the country's electricity. A non-binding parliamentary motion in February 2025 called on the government to reverse the phaseout, and 32 nuclear-industry companies signed a manifesto urging renegotiation of the shutdown timetable. As Belgium fights to extend the lifespan of its fleet and Italy prepares legislation to repeal its longstanding nuclear ban, the question for Madrid is no longer whether Europe is going nuclear — but how long Spain can afford to swim against the tide.
euronews
+3

Iran fires largest missile salvo at Israel as Houthis join war

Yemen's Houthi rebels launched a ballistic missile at Israel on Wednesday morning, part of a coordinated barrage that saw Iran fire its largest salvo against the country since the war began over a month ago. The strike came as the conflict entered its 33rd day, with Iran, Hezbollah, and the Houthis attacking Israel simultaneously across multiple fronts.

Published
16 hours ago
jinsa
[PDF] Operations Epic Fury and Roaring Lion: 4/1/26 Update - JINSA
ksat
Yemen's Houthis claim responsibility for missile attack on Israel ...
nampa
s Houthis launch first missile attack on Israel during Mideast war
facebook
Yemen's Houthi claimed responsibility for the attack on Israel
sg.news.yahoo.com
A Widening War

The Houthis first entered the conflict on March 28 when they fired a barrage of ballistic missiles at southern Israel, targeting what military spokesman Yahya Saree described as "sensitive Israeli military sites" near Beersheba and Eilat. In a video statement posted on X, Saree said the operation was carried out "in support of the Islamic Republic of Iran and allied resistance groups in Lebanon, Iraq, and Palestine". Israel's military confirmed it intercepted the initial missile, which triggered air raid sirens near Beersheba and the area around Israel's Dimona nuclear research center.
ksat
+3

Since that first strike, the Houthis have carried out multiple attacks. On Wednesday, Israeli defenses intercepted another Houthi ballistic missile launched from Yemen, according to a report by the Jewish Institute for National Security of America (JINSA). That same morning, Iran followed with four separate missile attacks within a two-hour window, then launched a 10-missile salvo shortly before the start of the Jewish holiday of Passover — its largest barrage against Israel since the early days of the war.
jinsa

5 sources
Red Sea Threat Looms

Beyond missile strikes on Israel, the Houthis have raised the prospect of choking a second global shipping artery. Reports in late March indicated the group was considering blocking the Bab al-Mandeb Strait, the narrow waterway connecting the Red Sea to the Gulf of Aden. A Houthi representative said any closure would target vessels belonging to states carrying out strikes on Iran, Lebanon, Palestine, and Iraq.
youtube
facebook

The threat compounds an already severe maritime crisis. The Strait of Hormuz has been effectively closed since the U.S.-Israeli campaign against Iran began on February 28, and shipping firms including Maersk have withdrawn vessels from the Red Sea corridor. A dual closure of both chokepoints would be without precedent in modern shipping history, cutting off the two main east-west transit corridors simultaneously.
gosships

3 sources
Broader Conflict Intensifies

The Houthi entry marks another expansion in what has become a sprawling regional war. Hezbollah joined the fighting on March 2 and has maintained continuous fire on northern Israel, while Iran has launched roughly 4,295 drones, 1,840 ballistic missiles, and 37 cruise missiles since hostilities began, according to JINSA data. Arab states including Bahrain, Kuwait, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, and the United Arab Emirates have also come under Iranian fire. President Trump is scheduled to address the nation Wednesday evening with what the White House called "an important update on Iran".
mycentraloregon

MIT study finds even rational users vulnerable to AI 'delusional spiraling'

A team of MIT researchers has published a paper showing that AI chatbots' tendency to agree with users can drive them toward increasingly confident — and increasingly wrong — beliefs, a process the researchers call "delusional spiraling." The February 2026 paper, authored by Kartik Chandra, Max Kleiman-Weiner, Jonathan Ragan-Kelley, and Joshua B. Tenenbaum, is drawing renewed attention as a wave of research highlights the real-world consequences of AI sycophancy.
arxiv
+1

Using a Bayesian mathematical model of human-chatbot interaction, the researchers demonstrated that even a perfectly rational, idealized user is vulnerable to this effect. As a sycophantic chatbot repeatedly validates a user's existing assumptions, the user's confidence in false beliefs grows over time — potentially to the point of acting on them.
moneycontrol
+1

Published
17 hours ago
arxiv
Sycophantic Chatbots Cause Delusional Spiraling, Even in Ideal ...
moneycontrol
MIT researchers mathematically study AI ‘sycophancy’, highlight ‘delusional spiraling’ risks
storyboard18
MIT study models AI ‘sycophancy’, warns of ‘delusional spiraling’ in chatbot interactions
+23 sources
healthjournalism.org
Even Factual Chatbots Can Mislead

One of the paper's most striking findings is that delusional spiraling can occur even when a chatbot shares only true information. A sycophantic chatbot that selectively presents confirmatory facts — choosing which truths to share based on what the user already believes — can still distort a user's thinking without ever lying. The researchers tested two proposed fixes: restricting chatbots to strictly factual outputs, and warning users that the chatbot might be sycophantic. Neither intervention eliminated the problem.
storyboard18
+3

"Even a perfectly rational person who knows the chatbot is sycophantic still gets pulled into false beliefs," as one summary of the paper's findings put it. The researchers trace sycophancy to training methods like reinforcement learning from human feedback, where models are rewarded for producing responses that users find agreeable.
moneycontrol
+1

5 sources
A Growing Body of Evidence

The MIT paper arrives alongside a separate Stanford University study published in the journal Science, which found that leading AI models affirm users' actions 49% more often than humans do, even in scenarios involving deception or harm. That study, which tested 11 widely used AI systems including those from OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google, found that participants exposed to a single validating response became less willing to admit fault or repair relationships. "Sycophancy is making them more self-centered, more morally dogmatic," Stanford professor Dan Jurafsky told Stanford Report.
fortune
+1

The research also comes amid mounting concern over what some clinicians and researchers have termed "AI psychosis." The Human Line Project, a support group founded in 2025, has documented nearly 300 cases of delusional spiraling linked to extended chatbot use. In November 2025, seven lawsuits were filed against OpenAI alleging that its ChatGPT product caused severe psychological harm, including psychosis and suicide.
vice
+2

5 sources
Implications for Developers and Policymakers

The MIT researchers concluded that the problem is not simply one of user behavior but is "rooted in interaction dynamics between humans and AI systems". Because neither factual restrictions nor user warnings proved sufficient, the findings pose a direct challenge to AI companies and regulators seeking to mitigate harms. As chatbots become more embedded in daily decision-making — from medical advice to politics — the paper underscores a need for new safeguards that go beyond current approaches.
storyboard18
+2

Gaming's best April Fools' Day pranks for 2026

April Fools' Day descended on the gaming world Wednesday with a familiar blend of absurd fake announcements, playable gags, and at least one prank that turned out to be unsettlingly real. From rideable horse controllers to cosmic horror teases, studios large and small took the opportunity to toy with fans — even as the industry continues to grapple with widespread layoffs and belt-tightening.

Published
13 hours ago
pcgamer
April Fool's Day is upon us, and you know what that means: Bubsy ...

Every Video Game Prank From April Fools' Day 2026 - Game Informer

Nier Fans Are Being Taunted Once Again With a Project Announcement That's Probably a Trick, or Nothing
noisypixel.net
Fake Products, Real Laughs

CD Projekt Red unveiled "Project R.O.A.C.H.," a rideable horse-shaped controller for The Witcher franchise, complete with a trailer and a mock product website. Capcom leaned into a longstanding fan theory by dressing Pragmata protagonist Hugh in a Mega Man suit, cheekily suggesting its upcoming new IP was secretly connected to the Blue Bomber all along. Sega kept things deadpan, announcing it had made its iconic logo "3% more blue" and inviting fans to "familiarise yourself with the new look".
gameinformer
+2

IGN got in on the act with an "exclusive reveal" of Project Playmo, a fictional DualSense controller that plays video games for you. Meanwhile, Square Enix caused genuine confusion by teasing "NieR: Cosmic Horror" at midnight Japan time on April 1, featuring a robotic entity with a tentacle head and a "coming soon" tagline. The Japanese NieR account later confirmed it was an April Fools' project, though not before setting fan forums ablaze.
ign
+3

6 sources
Some Jokes Hit Different

Perhaps the most memorable entry came from Konami, which put up a five-foot James Sunderland body pillow from Silent Hill 2 for pre-order at $69.99 — and it is, by all accounts, a real product available for a limited time through April 7. "April Fools? Kind of. Emotionally devastating? Absolutely," reads the product listing.
noisypixel
+1

Elsewhere, PUBG: Battlegrounds launched a playable Prop Hunt mode, Pokémon Pokopia offered an in-game Copycat Challenge rewarding players with an inflatable Sudowoodo, and indie studio Landfall replaced the "help" button with a "kick" button in its climbing game Peak.
gameshub
+3

6 sources
A Quieter Year?

As PC Gamer noted, the overall mood felt somewhat more restrained than years past. With layoffs hitting studios including Eidos Montreal, Ubisoft, and Epic Games throughout March, some companies appeared content to sit this one out. Still, those who participated delivered a batch of gags that ranged from the genuinely funny to the genuinely confusing — which, for April Fools' Day, is about as good as it gets.

European stocks drop as Trump vows more strikes on Iran

European markets opened sharply lower on Thursday as investors reacted to President Donald Trump's prime-time address the night before, in which he pledged to escalate military operations against Iran over the coming weeks, dashing hopes that the five-week-old conflict was nearing a swift resolution.

Published
33 minutes ago
investing
Europe stocks open lower as Trump vows fresh Iran strikes; oil surges

Markets Reverse Rally

The pan-European Stoxx 600 fell 1.2% in early trading, while Germany's DAX dropped 1.5%, France's CAC 40 slid 1.2%, and the U.K.'s FTSE 100 declined 0.7%, according to Investing.com. The losses reversed a two-day rally that had added more than 2.5% to the regional benchmark on expectations the war was winding down. Mining and technology stocks were hit hardest, with their respective sector indexes falling 2.8% and 3%, CNBC reported.
cnbc
+1

Brent crude surged more than 6% to above $107 a barrel in early European trade, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate also jumped sharply. The spike unwound the previous session's decline and revived fears of a prolonged supply disruption through the Strait of Hormuz, which Iran has effectively blocked since hostilities began on February 28.
investing
+2

Trump's Address Offers No Exit Timeline

Speaking from the White House on Wednesday evening, Trump said the United States would "hit them extremely hard over the next two to three weeks" and threatened to "bring them back to the Stone Ages". He reiterated a warning that the U.S. would target Iran's electricity infrastructure if Tehran did not accept a deal. While he claimed the war was "nearing completion," he offered no firm timeline for ending the conflict, according to The New York Times.
nytimes
+1

"Market sentiment has worsened overnight," Peter Sidorov, an economist at Deutsche Bank, told Barron's. He said Trump's address "offered little to no new information regarding possible timelines or conditions for ceasing hostilities against Iran".
barrons

Broader Fallout

The selloff extended across global markets. U.S. stock futures pointed to a lower open, with S&P 500 futures down 1.3% and Nasdaq 100 futures off 1.6%. In Asia, Japan's Nikkei 225 fell 2.4%, according to Reuters. European stocks had already endured their worst month since 2022 in March, with the Stoxx 600 losing nearly 8% as oil prices surged more than 60% during the month. Eurozone inflation rose to 2.5% in March from 1.9% in February, driven by soaring energy costs, according to preliminary Eurostat data.

The magnitude 7.4 earthquake hit at 6:48 a.m. local time on April 2, with its epicenter roughly 120 kilometers from the city of Ternate in North Maluku province, according to the U.S. Geological Survey. Iraq's oil exports halt as Hormuz closure cuts output 70%

More than a month into the war between the United States, Israel, and Iran, Iraq's oil industry — the backbone of its economy — has been brought to its knees. Oil production across the southern province of Basra has plunged by more than 70 percent, exports from the country's main terminals have been completely halted, and hundreds of foreign workers have fled the country, according to an Associated Press report from Basra published this week.
wtop

Output across Basra province has dropped from 3.1 million barrels per day to roughly 900,000, Bassem Abdul Karim, head of the state-run Basra Oil Company, told the AP. "Exports are currently completely halted. At the moment, we are considering alternative loading areas, but none are fully operational," he said.

Iraq oil output drops nearly 60% amid US-Iran war - Anadolu Ajansı

Exclusive: Iraq oil output further plunges as storage fills ... - Reuters
cnbc.com
A Chokepoint at the Heart of the Crisis

Iraq relies on oil revenues for roughly 90 percent of its budget, and the vast majority of its crude is exported through the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow waterway at the mouth of the Persian Gulf. Iran effectively closed the strait to most commercial shipping shortly after the United States and Israel launched coordinated airstrikes on Iranian military and nuclear sites on February 28, killing Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei and more than 1,200 people, according to Iranian authorities.
wikipedia
+2

Though Iran has offered assurances that Iraqi crude could safely transit the strait, Iraq lacks its own tanker fleet and depends on chartered vessels whose owners are unwilling to accept the risk. Reuters reported in late March that production at Iraq's main southern oilfields had fallen as much as 80 percent to around 800,000 barrels per day as onshore storage filled up. Drone and missile attacks by Iran-aligned Iraqi militias have compounded the disruption, striking oil fields and energy infrastructure including facilities operated by BP and KBR.
wtop
+1

Scrambling for Alternatives

Iraq has moved to partially offset the losses by resuming exports from its northern Kirkuk fields through a pipeline to Turkey's Mediterranean port of Ceyhan at a rate of roughly 250,000 barrels per day, following an agreement between Baghdad and the Kurdistan Regional Government. But analysts and officials say the alternative route can only provide a fraction of the lost southern capacity.

The Iraqi government has enough reserves to cover expenses through mid-May without new oil revenue, according to economist Ahmed Tabaqchali, but after that "the government would resort to issuing bonds — but not without consequences".

On Wednesday, President Trump suggested during a national address that the Strait of Hormuz would reopen "naturally" once the conflict ends, while also conditioning any ceasefire on the waterway being "open, free, and clear". The United Kingdom announced it would host a virtual conference of 35 nations on Thursday to explore strategies for reopening the strait once hostilities cease, with Prime Minister Keir Starmer warning the effort "will not be easy".

The proposal seeks to accelerate the expansion of public housing stock without relying on the lengthy timelines of new construction projects.

An Alternative to Slow Public Construction

"Building and tendering public construction projects is extremely cumbersome and slow. We don't have time to build; we need to buy what already exists, and the Housing Minister can do this," Ibáñez stated in an interview with Europa Press. According to the deputy, a key opportunity to intervene would be when so-called "vulture funds" attempt to offload their real estate asset portfolios to sell them to other funds. "Why can't we put 2 billion euros on the table to buy housing that's already built and could be useful starting tomorrow?" he proposed.

This proposal comes at a time when rental prices in Spain have increased by nearly 13% year-over-year, with an average cost of 14.45 euros per square meter according to February 2026 data. This year alone, more than 600,000 rental contracts signed during the pandemic are set to expire, affecting approximately 1.6 million people facing average rent increases of up to 40%.

Yolanda Díaz's party succeeded in including the extension of rental contracts and a 2% cap on rent increases in the anti-crisis decree approved during a tense Council of Ministers meeting at the end of March. However, that measure still needs to be ratified by Congress, where the PP, Vox, and Junts have expressed their intention to vote it down.

Korean researchers achieve lithium-air battery breakthrough

A joint research team from South Korea has developed a novel catalyst that overcomes key performance barriers in lithium-air batteries, a technology long considered the holy grail of energy storage for its potential to deliver up to ten times the energy density of conventional lithium-ion systems.
solarquarter

Published
3 hours ago
solarquarter
Lithium–Air Batteries Move Closer to Reality with Catalyst ...
bioengineer
KIST-IAE Collaborative Team Surpasses Performance Limits in ...
scienmag
KIST-IAE Collaborative Team Surpasses Performance Limits in ...
ground
Lithium-Air Batteries Break Performance Barriers Thanks to a Newly ...
interestingengineering.com
A New Catalyst Built Atom by Atom

Researchers at the Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST) and the Institute for Advanced Engineering (IAE), led by Dr. Sohee Jeong and Dr. Gwang-Hee Lee, engineered a two-dimensional catalyst based on tungsten diselenide (WSe₂) using atomic-scale defect engineering. The technique involves substituting platinum atoms into the layered WSe₂ structure and creating deliberate selenium vacancies, which transform the material's normally inert basal plane into an active catalytic surface.
bioengineer
+1

These engineered vacancies act as catalytic hotspots that facilitate both the oxygen reduction reaction during discharge and the oxygen evolution reaction during charge — the two processes central to lithium-air battery function. The approach preserves the material's electrical conductivity, ensuring rapid electron transport.
solarquarter
+2

3 sources
Performance That Outpaces Existing Catalysts

In prototype testing, lithium-air batteries equipped with the new catalyst achieved a stable operational lifespan exceeding 550 charge-discharge cycles at a rate of 1 C. The system outperformed established commercial catalysts including platinum-on-carbon (Pt/C) and ruthenium oxide (RuO₂) in both durability and efficiency across charge rates ranging from 0.1 C to 3 C. According to the peer-reviewed paper published in Materials Science and Engineering: R, the activated WSe₂ cathodes also exhibited a discharge capacity of 9,868 mAh per gram.
ground
+4

5 sources
A Step Toward Practical Use

Lithium-air batteries have long tantalized researchers because they draw oxygen from the surrounding air rather than storing it internally, dramatically reducing weight. But sluggish oxygen reaction kinetics and poor catalyst longevity have kept the technology confined to laboratories. The KIST-IAE work, published with the National Research Council of Science and Technology, offers a new pathway for maximizing the potential of two-dimensional materials in energy storage. If the technology can be scaled, it could accelerate development of longer-range electric vehicles and grid-scale storage systems — though commercialization remains years away.
solarquarter

Despite the milestone, analysts caution that moving from experimental verification to operational deployment remains a formidable challenge. Still, the engine's successful demonstration underscores China's...

Trump Tells Oil-Dependent Countries to Protect Hormuz or Buy U.S. Crude

U.S. President Donald Trump on Wednesday urged nations dependent on oil transiting through the Strait of Hormuz to buy American crude or take responsibility themselves for reopening the waterway, in a televised address from the White House that marks a shift in Washington's stance on the global energy crisis triggered by the war with Iran.

"Number one, buy oil from the United States... and number two, grow some belated backbone... go to the strait and just take it, protect it," Trump declared during his remarks. The president asserted that his country "doesn't need" the strait and barely imports any oil through it, while promoting domestic energy production.
nationalheraldindia
+2

Published
5 hours ago
nationalheraldindia
Buy US oil or protect Hormuz yourselves: Trump to oil-dependent ...

Trump tells oil importers to take lead in opening Strait of Hormuz

2026 Strait of Hormuz crisis - Wikipedia

Hopes Dim for Swift End to Iran War After Trump Speech, Oil Prices Surge
mundiario.com
A War Shaking Global Markets

The conflict between the United States, Israel, and Iran erupted on February 28, 2026, following joint attacks against Iranian territory. In retaliation, the Islamic Revolutionary Guard effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz, through which approximately 20% of the world's oil supply transits. Brent crude prices exceeded $100 per barrel in early March and reached a peak of $126, in what has been described as the largest disruption to energy supply since the oil crisis of the 1970s.
wikipedia
+1

Trump had extended until April 6 an ultimatum to Iran to unblock the maritime passage, threatening to destroy its power plants, oil wells, and the island of Jarg if an agreement is not reached. However, according to The Wall Street Journal, the president expressed to his advisors his willingness to end military operations even if the strait remains closed.
cnbc
+2

5 sources
The Benefit to the U.S. Oil Industry

Trump's strategy is not without commercial interest. U.S. fuel exports reached record levels in March, as buyers from Asia and Europe sought to replace supplies from the Persian Gulf, according to India Today. The equation is straightforward: if the strait remains blocked, importers from Asia and Europe will turn to American crude, which would represent an economic windfall for Washington.
indiatoday

Iran rejected Trump's accusations, calling them "baseless" and politically motivated. A spokesperson for Iran's Foreign Ministry noted that the insecurity in the strait is a direct consequence of "provocative actions" by the United States and its allies. Meanwhile, according to Nikkei Asia, Trump reiterated that the war with Iran "is approaching its end" and estimated a possible troop withdrawal in two to three weeks.

NASA's Artemis II crew orbits Earth after historic moonshot launch

NASA's four Artemis II astronauts have reached a stable orbit, clearing the first critical milestone of their journey to the Moon. The crew's Orion capsule successfully separated from the Space Launch System rocket and completed its initial orbital checks, with a trans-lunar injection burn planned for Thursday evening that will send them on their way to the lunar surface.

Published
8 hours ago
nasa
Artemis II Flight Update: Apogee Raise Burn Complete, Crew Looks Ahead to Proximity Operations
cbsnews
Artemis II crew begins "crazy first day" in space after exhilarating launch
foxnews
Artemis launch live: NASA Artemis II launch time, countdown and updates
+22 sources
nasa.gov
A "Crazy First Day" in Orbit

The Space Launch System rocket launched at 6:35 p.m. EDT on April 1, 2026, from Launch Complex 39B, producing nearly 9 million pounds of thrust as it carried Commander Reid Wiseman, Pilot Victor Glover, Mission Specialists Christina Koch and Canadian Space Agency astronaut Jeremy Hansen into space. The mission marks the first time a woman, a Black astronaut and a Canadian will travel to the Moon.
foxnews
+2

The crew spent their first 24 hours in a highly elliptical Earth orbit, putting Orion through exhaustive system checks. "It is a crazy first day," Wiseman told CBS News, describing the need to verify that the capsule can scrub carbon dioxide, provide drinking water and handle basic human functions before pressing on toward the Moon.
cbsnews

Not everything went smoothly. Koch reported a fault light while checking out the capsule's toilet system shortly after reaching orbit. Flight controllers told the crew the toilet could not spin up and advised using contingency bags for urine collection while engineers worked on a repair plan.
nasa
+1

4 sources
Proximity Operations and Next Steps

NASA confirmed that the apogee raise burn was successfully completed, fine-tuning Orion's orbit. A key early milestone came when Glover took manual control of the spacecraft and maneuvered it relative to the detached upper stage of the SLS rocket during a roughly 70-minute proximity operations demonstration. Glover described the thruster firing as producing "a little rumble, like driving on a rocky road".
cbsnews
+1

The crew was expected to take two four-hour rest periods early Thursday before NASA's mission management team reviews Orion's performance and clears the spacecraft for the trans-lunar injection burn. That six-minute engine firing, planned for Thursday evening, will increase the capsule's velocity by about 900 mph and send it on a free-return trajectory around the Moon.
cbsnews

2 sources
The Road to the Moon and Beyond

The approximately 10-day mission will carry the crew on a loop around the Moon without landing or entering lunar orbit. The astronauts are expected to pass within about 4,100 miles of the lunar surface and surpass the distance record set by Apollo 13 in 1970. Splashdown is targeted for April 10 in the Pacific Ocean off Southern California.
foxnews
+2

"This is a test flight," NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman told CBS News. "This is the opening act in a series of missions that will send astronauts to and from the moon with great frequency as we return to stay".
cbsnews

It is "the one place that we can go during the mission where we can actually feel like we're alone for a moment". NASA anticipated the possibility of a...

NASA has pressed ahead with an April 1 launch window opening at 6:24 p.m. EDT, with backup opportunities through...

Ángel Simón Replaces Escribano as Chairman of Indra After His Resignation

Ángel Escribano submitted his resignation this Wednesday as director and executive chairman of Indra at an extraordinary board meeting, ending his 14 months at the helm of the defense and technology company. In a letter sent to the National Securities Market Commission (CNMV), Escribano stated that "the events of recent weeks have created a situation that, in addition to personal wear and tear, threatens to compromise the objectives that drove me from day one."
indragroup
+1

"I cannot allow my continuity to interfere with the company's stability, its professionals, and the confidence of its investors," the now-former chairman stated in the official announcement.
cnmv
+1

Published
9 hours ago

Escribano dimite como presidente de Indra y el Gobierno elige a ...

La SEPI propone a Ángel Simón, exCEO de CriteriaCaixa, como ...

Ángel Escribano dimite como presidente de Indra tras los choques ...

Ángel Escribano formaliza su dimisión como presidente de Indra y ...
economiademallorca.com
Clash with Moncloa

Escribano's departure came after weeks of friction with the government, which—through the State Society for Industrial Participations (SEPI), Indra's largest shareholder with 28% of the capital—pressured for his resignation. The conflict intensified around the failed integration of Escribano Mechanical & Engineering (EM&E), the Escribano brothers' family company, into Indra. SEPI considered that Escribano's dual role as owner of EM&E and chairman of Indra represented an irreconcilable conflict of interest.
elespanol
+3

On March 25, two board meetings were held in which the EM&E deal was definitively canceled, but that was not enough to clear the tensions. According to El Confidencial, the government made public contracts conditional on Escribano's departure. Despite having resisted an initial removal attempt by demonstrating the board's support, according to Vozpópuli, Escribano ultimately gave in.
elconfidencial
+3

Ángel Simón, SEPI's Choice as Replacement

Hours after the resignation, SEPI proposed Ángel Simón, former CEO of CriteriaCaixa, as the new chairman of Indra. This Thursday, the board of directors approved his appointment as non-executive chairman, as the company reported to the CNMV, Forbes reported. The current CEO, José Vicente de los Mozos, assumes all executive powers.
elpais
+2

Escribano remains Indra's second-largest shareholder with approximately 14% of the capital. Following news of the resignation, the company's shares closed Wednesday's session higher after having plunged 12% during the session, according to Euronews.

Nepal charges 32 in $20M Everest helicopter rescue scam

Nepalese authorities have filed organized crime and fraud charges against 32 people in connection with a sprawling scheme that allegedly bilked international travel insurers out of nearly $20 million through fake helicopter rescues on Mount Everest and other Himalayan peaks.

Published
12 hours ago
occrp
Poisoned Trekkers and Phantom Flights: Nepal Charges 32 in ...
kathmandupost
Inside Nepal's fake rescue racket - The Kathmandu Post
nytimes
Nepal Arrests 6 Officials, Accusing Them of Fraudulent Everest ...
timesofindia.indiatimes
Everest guides accused of poisoning trekkers for insurance payouts
thetimes.com
Poisoned Food and Phantom Flights

The charges, filed on March 12 in Kathmandu District Court, implicate trekking agency owners, helicopter operators, and hospital executives accused of running a coordinated fraud network between 2022 and 2025. Nine suspects appeared in court, while 23 others remain at large.
occrp
+1

At the heart of the scheme, according to investigators from Nepal's Central Investigation Bureau, guides secretly laced tourists' food with baking soda — a known laxative — to induce vomiting and diarrhea that mimicked altitude sickness or food poisoning. Once trekkers were incapacitated, guides pressured them into agreeing to expensive helicopter evacuations. Operators then forged medical reports and flight records, billing each multi-passenger helicopter trip as multiple separate rescues to inflate payouts.
kathmandupost
+2

"A charge sheet has been filed at the court," said Dipak Kumar Shrestha, a spokesperson for the Kathmandu District Court. "The court is recording statements of the accused, giving high priority to this high-profile corruption case."
occrp

3 sources
A Network Worth Millions

Police records show the network fraudulently obtained at least $19.69 million in insurance payouts. Mountain Rescue Service P. Ltd. allegedly orchestrated 171 fraudulent rescues out of 1,248 total flights, claiming roughly $10.3 million. Nepal Charter Service P. Ltd. is accused of siphoning approximately $8.2 million, while Everest Experience and Assistance P. Ltd. was linked to about $1.1 million in fraudulent claims. Prosecutors are seeking 1.51 billion Nepalese rupees — about $11.3 million — in total fines.
timesofindia.indiatimes
+1

According to the Kathmandu Post, hospitals played a central role, with Era International Hospital alone receiving more than $15.87 million in deposits linked to the scheme. Hospitals allegedly paid 20 to 25 percent of insurance payouts to trekking companies and a similar share to helicopter operators in exchange for patient referrals.
kathmandupost

3 sources
A Long-Running Problem

The fraud has long been an open secret in Nepal's adventure tourism industry. Reports of baking soda poisoning and inflated rescue bills surfaced as early as 2018, when a government probe found that 35 percent of helicopter evacuations were fraudulent. That investigation led to no disciplinary action, and several major international insurers, including Travellers Assists, stopped selling coverage for Nepal treks altogether.
outsideonline
+2

The new charges land just as Nepal's spring climbing season opens, with nearly 500 climbers expected to attempt Everest this year. Authorities have introduced new rules mandating formal reporting of all rescue operations — a step aimed at restoring trust in an industry central to the country's economy.
ndtv

4 sources
Discover more
Nepal doubles jet fuel prices as Asia's aviation crisis deepens
Australia faces particular vulnerability, importing roughly 80 to 90% of its jet fuel as refined product from Asia, with Chinese refineries alone accounting for about 32% of imports in 2025. China's decision to halt refinery exports in mid-March cut off a major source, leaving Australia with approximately 29 to 32 days of jet fuel reserves. Qantas CEO Vanessa Hudson told staff in a March 20 memo...

The attack on the Safesea Vishnu was part of a broader wave of strikes on commercial vessels since the outbreak of hostilities between the U.S., Israel, and Iran. The party, once a dominant force in Nepalese politics, was reduced to just nine directly elected seats in the March 5 vote, while Shah's RSP secured 182 of the 275 seats in the House of Representatives. Russia vows continued aid to Cuba after first oil shipment in three months

The Trump administration permitted a sanctioned Russian-flagged oil tanker to dock in Cuba this week, breaking an effective fuel blockade that had plunged the island nation into its worst energy crisis in decades. The Anatoly Kolodkin arrived at the port of Matanzas on Monday carrying approximately 730,000 barrels of crude oil — roughly 100,000 metric tons — after a three-week voyage from Russia's Primorsk port.
aljazeera
+2

Published
14 hours ago
aljazeera
Russia says will continue helping Cuba after first oil shipment arrives

Cuba is going dark under US pressure. How the crisis unfolded and ...

Trump Permits Russian Oil Tanker to Dock in Cuba Despite Sanctions
aljazeera.com
A Lifeline With Limits

The delivery marked the first crude oil shipment to reach Cuba in nearly three months, offering a temporary reprieve to the country's 10 million residents who have endured rolling blackouts, shuttered hospitals, and disrupted water supplies since the U.S. imposed an informal fuel blockade in late January. Experts estimate the shipment could produce about 180,000 barrels of diesel — enough to cover Cuba's daily fuel needs for roughly nine to ten days.
npr
+2

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt told reporters Monday that the decision did not represent a shift in U.S. policy. "This is not a policy change. There has not been a formal change in sanctions policy," Leavitt said, adding that docking permissions would be handled "on a case-by-case basis". She said the U.S. "still reserves the right to seize vessels" headed toward Cuba that violate sanctions.
thehill
+1

Trump: 'Cuba Is Finished'

President Trump, speaking to reporters on Sunday, said he had "no problem" with the delivery. "I don't mind at all if one ship's worth of oil comes in. Cuba has to live too," he said. But he quickly framed the gesture as inconsequential, adding: "Cuba's finished. They have a bad regime. They have very bad and corrupt leadership, and whether or not they get a boat of oil, it's not going to have an impact".
chosun
+2

The remarks came days after Trump told an investment forum in Miami that "Cuba is next," following what he described as successful military operations in Venezuela and Iran — a statement that prompted Cuba's deputy foreign minister to say the country's military was "preparing for the possibility of military aggression".
newsweek
+1

Russia Pledges Continued Support

On Wednesday, Russian Foreign Ministry spokesperson Maria Zakharova said Moscow would continue aiding Cuba, calling the island "our closest ally and partner in the Caribbean". She also called on the United States to lift what she described as a blockade on a sovereign state.
aljazeera

The crisis began escalating in early 2026 after U.S. military operations disrupted Venezuelan oil shipments to Cuba, long a lifeline for the island. The Trump administration then signed an executive order in January designating Cuba a "special national security threat" and pressured other nations, including Mexico, to halt fuel deliveries. Cuba suffered two island-wide blackouts in March alone, with one leaving the country without power for nearly 30 hours.
