BoE's Bailey says markets overpricing rate hikes amid energy shock

The synchronized rate-hiking playbook that defined the global response to the 2021-22 inflation surge is unlikely to repeat itself as a deepening energy shock from the war in the Middle East forces central banks into starkly different corners. Unlike five years ago, when every major G10 central bank except the Bank of Japan raised rates by 400 to 525 basis points in near-lockstep, the current crisis is exposing divergent economic conditions that could splinter policy responses across the Federal Reserve, the European Central Bank, and the Bank of England.

Published
16 hours ago
chathamfinancial
BoE and ECB keep rates on hold but both warn of inflation risks ...
globalbankingandfinance
J.P. Morgan sees only one BoE rate hike in 2026 after
money.usnews
Exclusive-Bank of England's Bailey Says Investors Should Not ...
money.usnews
BoE's Bailey Says Markets Are Getting Ahead of Themselves on ...
uk.finance.yahoo.com
Bailey Pushes Back as Markets Price in Hikes

In an exclusive Reuters interview published Wednesday, Bank of England Governor Andrew Bailey said financial markets were "still getting ahead of themselves" by pricing in interest rate increases in response to the Iran war's impact on the British economy. Markets are currently pricing in roughly two quarter-point rate hikes from the BoE this year, even as a majority of economists surveyed by Reuters expect rates to remain unchanged. J.P. Morgan responded Wednesday by cutting its forecast from two BoE hikes to just one, now expected in June.
globalbankingandfinance
+1

Bailey's pushback follows the BoE's decision on March 19 to hold rates at 3.75 percent, a move that came with a notably hawkish shift in tone as all four members who had previously voted for cuts switched to holding steady. "The right place to be is on hold," Bailey said at the time, adding that the central bank would need to assess whether higher energy costs risked spilling into broad-based inflation.
money.usnews
+2

5 sources
ECB and Fed Face Different Trade-Offs

The ECB also held rates unchanged at its March 19 meeting but revised its 2026 inflation forecast up to 2.6 percent from 2.0 percent in December, driven largely by higher energy prices. Major brokerages including Goldman Sachs and Barclays are now forecasting two ECB rate hikes this year, with some expecting the first as early as April. An ECB speech on March 25 outlined the bank's framework for responding, stressing that monetary policy "cannot bring down energy prices" but must act when higher energy costs risk spilling into entrenched inflation.
money.usnews
+3

The Federal Reserve, meanwhile, held its benchmark rate steady at 3.50 to 3.75 percent in March. Chair Jerome Powell said Monday that energy disruptions have "typically been fleeting" and that the Fed's policy is "well-positioned to observe how this unfolds". Markets have nearly ruled out rate cuts for 2026, with futures implying roughly a 48 percent probability of no cut at all this year.
morningstar
+2

7 sources
Divergence Ahead

A Reuters column by Jamie McGeever argued that the further the energy shock intensifies, the more central banks will act independently. Net energy exporters like Canada face different trade-offs — the Bank of Canada held rates at 2.25 percent in March, citing weaker growth and downside risks even as it pledged not to let the energy shock become "ingrained". UBS economists project that in a severe scenario with oil reaching $150 a barrel, the Fed could cut rates to zero while the ECB might not ease at all — a degree of divergence that would widen interest rate differentials, accelerate currency swings, and amplify market volatility.

BMI forecasts gold to stay under pressure amid hawkish Fed, inflation fears

Gold has retreated roughly 10% from its record-setting run in late January, trading near the $4,600 level as of April 1, as a hawkish Federal Reserve and oil-shock-driven inflation have combined to cool what had been the metal's most explosive rally in decades.

Published
21 hours ago
bloomberg
Silver Retreats After Best Jump Since 2008 as Gold Tops $5000
barchart
Gold Apr '26 Futures Price - Barchart.com
reuters
Gold blasts past $5,100 to record high on safe-haven rush | Reuters
thestreet
Gold's price is falling fast: Here's what comes next - TheStreet
cnbc.com
From Record Highs to a Sharp Pullback

On January 26, spot gold surged past $5,000 for the first time, reaching as high as $5,111 per ounce in a safe-haven rush fueled by escalating geopolitical tensions, according to Reuters. Bloomberg reported the intraday peak at $5,111.07 that day, and prices continued climbing through the final days of January, with futures touching above $5,600 by January 29, per Barchart data.
bloomberg
+2

The retreat began in earnest after the Federal Reserve held rates steady at 3.5% to 3.75% in March and signaled just one rate cut for 2026, a far more hawkish stance than markets had anticipated. The conflict between the U.S.-Israeli coalition and Iran, which began in late February, sent oil prices sharply higher — Goldman Sachs projected Brent crude averaging $115 a barrel in April — raising inflation expectations and further constraining the Fed's room to ease. The OECD warned that U.S. inflation could average 4.2% this year, more than a full percentage point above its prior forecast.
thestreet
+2

CNBC reported that the hawkish Fed tone, combined with a firmer U.S. dollar, capped gold's recovery attempts throughout March. By late March, gold had briefly dipped to around $4,100 before rebounding. As of March 31, spot gold stood at approximately $4,586, according to USA Today.
cnbc
+2

9 sources
Wall Street Stays Bullish Despite the Correction

Despite the selloff, major investment banks have maintained or raised their gold targets. JPMorgan set a year-end 2026 target of $6,300 per ounce, while Wells Fargo projected a range of $6,100 to $6,300, according to a report compiled by National Gold Group. Deutsche Bank reiterated its $6,000 target, and UBS forecast $6,200 by June. Reuters reported that JPMorgan also raised its long-term gold price forecast by 15% to $4,500 per ounce.
nationalgoldgroup
+1

BMI, a unit of Fitch Solutions, forecast gold averaging $4,600 for the full year of 2026 but warned that prices would "remain under pressure in Q2 2026 and beyond, with hawkish Fed sentiments and US dollar strength dampening gold's attractiveness".
informistmedia

3 sources
Structural Tailwinds Persist

The longer-term case for gold rests on continued central bank purchases and de-dollarization trends. The World Gold Council noted that central banks from Guatemala, Indonesia, and Malaysia recently entered or re-entered the gold market, and projected official sector purchases of around 850 metric tons this year. State Street Global Advisors described central bank buying as "price inelastic," providing a steady demand floor that dampens downside volatility.
thehindubusinessline
+1

Technical analysts are watching the $4,400 to $4,600 zone as a critical support range. "As long as price is trading above $4,400, technically gold is safe and the bull market can continue," one analyst noted in a widely followed technical briefing. A sustained break below that level would be needed to challenge the broader uptrend that has been in place since 2023.
youtube

Over 100 law experts warn U.S. strikes on Iran may be war crimes

More than 100 U.S.-based international law experts, professors, and practitioners issued a joint letter on Thursday warning that American military strikes on Iran violate the United Nations Charter and may constitute war crimes, adding legal weight to a growing chorus of international opposition to the five-week-old conflict.
justsecurity
+1

Published
2 hours ago
justsecurity
Over 100 International Law Experts Warn: U.S. Strikes on Iran Violate UN Charter and May Be War Crimes
justsecurity
Over 100 International Law Experts Warn: U.S. Strikes on Iran ...
timesofisrael
To some, legally problematic strikes on Iran clear a higher bar: morality
sites.duke
Three independent justifications for the US/Israeli operations against ...
dawnmena.org
The Legal Case

The letter, published by Just Security, addresses violations of both the UN Charter's prohibition on the use of force and international humanitarian law. The experts join UN Secretary-General António Guterres, who condemned the strikes as illegal at the UN Security Council shortly after the U.S. and Israel launched "Operation Epic Fury" on February 28. The U.S.-Israeli campaign killed Iranian Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and struck more than 1,000 targets across Iran.
timesofisrael
+2
youtube
justsecurity

Critics have argued the strikes amount to an illegal war of aggression under international law. "This is the use of force on Iranian territory that appears, at first glance, contrary to the United Nations Charter," said Sébastien Touzé, a professor of international law at Panthéon-Assas University. Alain Pellet, a professor at the University of Nanterre and counsel before the International Court of Justice, said the strikes are "neither preventive nor preemptive" and are "most often used to disguise unlawful recourse under international law — acts of aggression or armed intervention". U.S.-Israeli strikes have killed at least 1,574 civilians in Iran, including 236 children, according to the Human Rights Activist News Agency.
lemonde
+1

7 sources
Oil Market Chaos and Global Pressure

The legal warning comes amid what the International Energy Agency described in March as "the largest supply disruption in the history of the global oil market". Iran's control of the Strait of Hormuz — through which roughly 20% of the world's traded petroleum normally passes — has effectively choked global energy supplies down to "a trickle," the IEA said. Gulf oil-producing nations have been forced to cut output by at least 10 million barrels per day as storage reaches capacity, and the IEA coordinated an unprecedented release of 400 million barrels from strategic reserves.
bloomberg
+3

4 sources
Diplomatic Push

On March 31, China and Pakistan launched a five-point peace initiative calling for "the immediate cessation of hostilities" and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. The joint proposal, announced after talks between Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi and Pakistani Deputy Prime Minister Ishaq Dar in Beijing, also called for protection of civilians and critical infrastructure, adherence to international humanitarian law, and the start of peace talks. China's Foreign Ministry said the initiative is "open" and welcomed participation from all countries. The diplomatic effort follows a separate quadrilateral call for an end to the war from Pakistan, Saudi Arabia, Turkey, and Egypt.
rferl
+4

5 sources
Discover more
HRW says U.S., Israel, and Iran are openly flouting laws of war
HRW described the conflict's geographic spread, speed of escalation, and open disregard for international norms as "a critical stress test for the international legal order created to protect civilians during armed conflict". The group urged allies of all warring parties to speak out, suspend complicit military assistance, and pursue accountability — warning that impunity for past crimes in...

As the war enters its fourth week, a new AP-NORC poll released Tuesday found that about 59% of Americans say US military action against Iran has gone too far, including roughly nine in 10 Democrats and six in 10 independents. About 45% said they are "extremely" or "very" concerned about affording gasoline in coming months, up from 30% when Trump won reelection. Trump's overall approval rating...

Yet the conflict has only escalated, with Iran's foreign minister warning of "zero restraint" if energy infrastructure is targeted again, and Iran's parliament speaker threatening that regional oil and energy...

Rachel VanLandingham, a professor at Southwestern Law School and retired U.S. Air Force lieutenant colonel who served as chief legal adviser for international law at U.S. Central Command, said that deliberately causing excessive civilian suffering through such strikes constitutes a war crime under international law. Ethereum drops sharply after Trump vows to escalate Iran war

Ethereum fell sharply on Thursday after President Donald Trump's primetime address on the Iran war Wednesday night dashed hopes of an imminent ceasefire, triggering a wave of selling across crypto derivatives markets. More than $1 billion in ETH sell volume flooded derivatives exchanges within a single hour of Trump's remarks, with $968 million of that concentrated on Binance alone, according to CryptoQuant analyst Darkfost.
mexc

ETH dropped to around $2,046, underperforming Bitcoin, which fell 3.11% over the same period. Solana declined 5.62%. The selloff came after markets had rallied for two days on expectations that Trump's first national address since the conflict began would signal de-escalation.
mexc

Published
7 minutes ago
mexc
Why Ethereum Took a Bigger Hit Than Bitcoin After Trump's Iran ...
aljazeera
Trump's primetime speech on Iran war: Key takeaways - Al Jazeera
youtube
Trump says US forces will 'finish the job' in Iran soon while speaking ...
apnews
Read the complete transcript of Trump's address to the nation
pbs.org
Trump's Speech Fuels Risk-Off Rotation

Instead of offering a path to peace, Trump told the nation Wednesday night that the United States would "hit them extremely hard over the next two to three weeks" and threatened to bomb Iran "back to the Stone Ages," according to a transcript published by the Associated Press. He vowed to "finish the job" while offering no specifics on how the conflict would conclude or any plan to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
aljazeera
youtube
apnews

The reaction across financial markets was swift. The S&P 500 shed hundreds of billions in market capitalization within minutes, oil surged, and Treasury bonds rallied as investors rotated into safety. Asian stocks opened lower, and gold fell as broader risk sentiment soured.
barchart
+1

5 sources
Why Ethereum Bears the Brunt

Analysts say Ethereum's steeper decline reflects its structural vulnerability during geopolitical shocks. Unlike Bitcoin, which carries a partial "digital gold" safe-haven narrative, Ethereum trades as a high-beta risk asset — closer to a leveraged tech stock than a store of value. When institutional desks rotate to safety, ETH is typically sold first. The concentration of leveraged long positions on Binance created conditions for a liquidation cascade, with forced selling amplifying the move beyond what broader market conditions warranted.
mexc

The CMC Fear and Greed Index sits at 27, firmly in "Fear" territory, having only partially recovered from a yearly low of 15 hit last month.
mexc

1 source
On-Chain Activity Offers a Counterpoint

Despite the price decline, Ethereum's network activity tells a different story. Data from Santiment shows daily active addresses have reached 788,000, with the network adding roughly 255,000 new wallet addresses per day — levels approaching historical highs. Yahoo Finance reported that the figures suggest user engagement has not diminished during the selloff.
weex
+2

The disconnect between falling prices and rising adoption underscores a familiar tension in crypto: short-term derivatives-driven volatility pulling in one direction while long-term network fundamentals point in another. Until the Strait of Hormuz reopens and the broader geopolitical picture clarifies, that tension is unlikely to resolve.
investing

The crypto market's trajectory remains closely tied to the Iran conflict and its knock-on effects on energy prices and global risk appetite. U.S. gas prices have hit $4 a gallon — a level not seen since 2022 — and the Strait of Hormuz remains largely closed. A Wall Street Journal report indicated Trump privately told advisors he wants a ceasefire within four to six weeks, even if the strait...

Chainalysis data shows a surge in Bitcoin withdrawals to personal wallets during recent protest waves, as citizens seek to preserve savings outside a failing banking system. South Korea's Lee urges citizens to 'save every drop of fuel' as Hormuz crisis deepens

Asian governments are deploying an increasingly urgent mix of austerity measures, emergency budgets, and diplomatic maneuvering as the closure of the Strait of Hormuz — through which roughly 20 percent of the world's oil flows — enters its second month with no resolution in sight.

South Korean President Lee Jae Myung on Thursday urged citizens to "save every drop of fuel," warning in a parliamentary address that the Middle East crisis has triggered one of the most severe energy security threats in decades. His government finalized a 26.2 trillion won (approximately $17.3 billion) supplementary budget on Tuesday, with 10.1 trillion won earmarked specifically for easing the burden of elevated oil prices through fuel subsidies, consumer vouchers, and a petroleum price ceiling. The National Assembly is expected to approve the package by April 10.
pulse.mk
+4

Published
2 hours ago
wikipedia
2026 Philippine energy crisis - Wikipedia
bworldonline
Manila seeks Iran assurance on oil transit - BusinessWorld Online
newsinfo.inquirer
Possible fuel price cap, rationing raised in Senate's initial report
bworldonline
Senator calls for oil price cap, rationing - BusinessWorld Online
uk.news.yahoo.com
Rationing and Price Caps in the Philippines

The Philippines, which imports up to 98 percent of its oil from the Middle East, is confronting perhaps the starkest supply threat in the region. A Senate ad hoc committee led by Senator Sherwin Gatchalian on Wednesday recommended that the Department of Energy study the imposition of temporary fuel price caps and rationing to extend dwindling reserves and prevent hoarding. Diesel prices have surged to roughly 140 pesos per liter after a fourth consecutive week of sharp increases. President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. declared a national energy emergency on March 24, and Manila is now seeking diplomatic assurances from Iran for safe passage of Philippine-bound tankers through the strait.
wikipedia
+3

4 sources
Beijing Orders Refiners to Absorb Losses

In China, the National Development and Reform Commission told executives at private refineries this week that securing domestic fuel supply is the top priority, directing them to maintain gasoline and diesel output at 2025 levels — even at a financial loss, according to Bloomberg. Independent refiners that cut production will face reductions in future oil import quotas, the people familiar with the discussions said. The directive comes as private refinery run rates fell below 63 percent of capacity in the week to April 1, the lowest since August 2025.
businesstimes
+1

2 sources
Alternative Supplies and Conservation

Several energy-starved Asian nations have turned to Russian crude under a 30-day US sanctions waiver issued March 12 by Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent to "promote stability in global energy markets". The Philippines recently received its first cargo of Russian ESPO crude in nearly six years, while South Korea's first Russian naphtha shipment of the year arrived at Daesan port.
gtreview
+2

Malaysia, meanwhile, announced that public-sector employees and staff at government-linked companies will work from home starting April 15 to reduce fuel consumption. "The cabinet has agreed to the work-from-home policy. It aims to reduce fuel consumption and ensure a stable energy supply," Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim said. The government also cut its monthly fuel subsidy quota from 300 liters to 200 liters per person.
marketwatch
+2

The sanctions waiver expires April 11, and analysts warn that if the strait remains effectively closed past mid-April, the supply disruptions — already described as the largest since the 1970s energy crisis — will deepen further.

Vance engaged Pakistan intermediaries on Iran ceasefire, Reuters reports

Vice President JD Vance has been privately engaging Pakistani intermediaries about the ongoing Iran conflict as recently as Tuesday, according to a source briefed on the matter cited by Reuters. At President Donald Trump's direction, Vance signaled that Trump was open to a ceasefire as long as certain U.S. demands were met, including the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, the source told Reuters on Wednesday.
iranintl
+1

Vance also delivered what the source described as a "stern message" that Trump was growing impatient, warning of increasing pressure on Iranian infrastructure unless Tehran agreed to a deal. Pakistan has been acting as an intermediary between the United States and Iran throughout the now five-week-old conflict.
globalbankingandfinance
+1

Published
21 hours ago
bangordailynews
Iran war raises 2028 stakes as Trump weighs Vance vs. Rubio
cnn
Vance, Rubio and others in Trump's inner circle preached caution ...
abcnews
Trump says he and Vance are 'philosophically' different on Iran war
apnews
Trump says Vance 'philosophically' different on Iran but ... - AP News
abcnews.com
A Cautious Voice in a Hawkish Administration

Vance's expanding diplomatic role underscores an ideological divide at the heart of the Trump administration. The vice president, a former Marine and longtime critic of foreign military entanglements, initially expressed reservations about launching strikes against Iran, according to ABC News. Once it became clear Trump had decided to move forward, Vance shifted to supporting the operation but pushed for a swift, limited campaign.
cnn
+1

Trump himself acknowledged the tension publicly on March 9, telling reporters at his Doral, Florida, golf club that Vance was "philosophically a little bit different than me" at the outset of the war, though he dismissed any suggestion of a rift, according to the Associated Press. Trump said Vance was "maybe less enthusiastic about going" but maintained the strikes were necessary.
apnews

Secretary of State Marco Rubio, by contrast, has aligned himself closely with Trump's hawkish stance and emerged as one of the administration's most vocal defenders of the military campaign. Rubio told PBS the war could end "in a matter of weeks".
pbs
+1

5 sources
2028 Shadows

The divergence between the two men is already shaping the 2028 Republican presidential primary. Both are widely viewed as potential successors to Trump, and their contrasting postures on Iran have become a proxy for the broader ideological struggle over the party's future. A recent CPAC straw poll showed Vance still leading at 53 percent but down from 61 percent a year earlier, while Rubio surged to 35 percent from just 3 percent.
channelnewsasia
+1
youtube

White House officials have told Axios that Vance's senior standing and opposition to prolonged overseas conflicts made him a more appealing interlocutor for Iran than other U.S. envoys, with Middle East envoy Steve Witkoff himself recommending Vance as lead negotiator. One senior administration official put it bluntly: if Tehran cannot reach an agreement with Vance, "they don't get a deal".
tribune
+1

Alcoa, Century Aluminum surge after EGA smelter halt confirmed

Shares of Alcoa and Century Aluminum surged on Wednesday after Bloomberg reported that Emirates Global Aluminium, the Middle East's largest aluminum producer, had fully halted operations at its Al Taweelah smelter following Iranian missile and drone strikes that struck the facility over the weekend. The disclosure intensified fears of a prolonged supply disruption in global aluminum markets, sending investors rushing into North American producers poised to benefit from reduced Gulf output.
bloomberg
+1

According to Bloomberg, the smelter on the outskirts of Abu Dhabi lost power during the attacks and its potlines — the smelting circuits where aluminum is produced — were forced into an uncontrolled shutdown, causing metal to solidify inside the equipment and inflicting deep damage to the facility. The report, citing a person familiar with the matter, indicated the extent of the operational halt had not been previously disclosed by EGA.
bloomberg

Published
19 hours ago
newsweek
Iran Strike on Key Aluminum Producer Threatens Global Supply ...
energynews.oedigital
Wood Mac, a consultancy, says that EGA's Al Taweelah aluminum ...
cnbc
Alumninum prices rise after Iran attacks Gulf smelters - CNBC
barchart
Up 53% in 2026, Does Century Aluminum Stock Have Any Room ...
newsweek.com
A Supply Shock Ripples Through Markets

The strikes, which took place Saturday as part of broader Iranian attacks on the Khalifa Economic Zone Abu Dhabi, also damaged Aluminium Bahrain's facility, which Wood Mackenzie estimated would operate at roughly 30% utilization going forward. EGA CEO Abdulnasser Bin Kalban said in a statement that several employees were injured but none fatally, and that the company was assessing damage to its facilities.
newsweek
+2

Alcoa shares had already rallied roughly 11% on Monday following the initial reports of damage to Gulf smelters. Century Aluminum, the largest primary aluminum smelter in the United States, gained 7.3% on Monday and was up more than 53% for the year heading into Wednesday's session. Both stocks extended gains Wednesday as the Bloomberg report confirmed the full operational halt.
barchart
+2

6 sources
Global Supply Concerns Deepen

The Gulf Cooperation Council countries produced approximately 6.16 million metric tons of primary aluminum in 2025, representing about 8% to 9% of global output, according to the International Aluminium Institute. In the ex-China market, however, the GCC's share rises to roughly 23%, making the region far more critical to Western supply chains. GCC exports accounted for approximately 21% of total U.S. primary aluminum imports in 2025, according to S&P Global, which noted that Gulf shipments had helped offset reduced Canadian supply following the imposition of 50% import tariffs.
spglobal
+2

Aluminum prices on the London Metal Exchange climbed sharply in recent sessions, with cash settlement prices rising from $3,292 per metric ton on March 27 to $3,585 on March 31. ING warned in a recent analysis that prolonged disruption in the region could push aluminum prices above $4,000 per ton, noting that Middle Eastern smelters hold only three to four weeks of alumina inventories and are heavily reliant on imported raw materials.
westmetall
+1

Alcoa is scheduled to release its first-quarter 2026 earnings on April 16.
stocktitan

China flips record LNG volumes as war chokes Asian gas supply

Chinese companies have resold a record 1.31 million metric tonnes of liquefied natural gas so far this year, including eight to ten cargoes reloaded in March alone — the highest monthly total ever recorded — as the war in the Middle East transforms the world's largest LNG importer into a lucrative middleman for its fuel-starved neighbors.
united24media
+1

The resold cargoes were delivered primarily to South Korea, Thailand, Japan, India, and the Philippines, according to data from Kpler, Vortexa, and ICIS cited by Reuters. The year-to-date volume already exceeds all LNG that Chinese firms resold in the entirety of 2025 or 2023.
oilprice
+1

Published
14 hours ago
shipuniverse
Asia's LNG Shock Turns March Into the Sharpest Import Pullback ...
markets.chroniclejournal
Crude Oil Tops $110 as Strait of Hormuz Closure Sparks Global ...

Asian Prices May Surpass $26 After Strike on Qatar LNG Plant
wikipedia
2026 Iran war - Wikipedia
oilprice.com
A War-Driven Arbitrage

The opportunity was created by the Strait of Hormuz closure and damage to Qatar's Ras Laffan complex — home to the world's largest LNG plant — following the U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran that began on February 28. QatarEnergy declared force majeure and halted LNG shipments, removing a substantial share of global supply from the market.
markets.chroniclejournal
+3

Asian spot LNG prices, measured by the Japan-Korea Marker, surged from around $11 per million British thermal units before the conflict to above $24 in early March, according to S&P Global Platts. Prices have since fluctuated but remained elevated, with the JKM settling near $20 per million BTU at the end of March. Analysts at ICIS warned prices could exceed $30 if the Strait remains closed for three months.
finance.yahoo
+3

Rather than compete for expensive spot cargoes, Chinese buyers leaned on domestic gas production, pipeline deliveries from Russia, and existing inventories. That left contracted LNG volumes available for resale at a steep markup to desperate regional buyers.
oilprice
+1

9 sources
China's Own Imports Crater

China's March LNG imports fell to roughly 3.68 million tonnes, the lowest monthly level since April 2018 and a decline of about 25% from a year earlier, according to Kpler shipping data cited by Bloomberg. The drop extended a months-long pullback in Chinese buying that predated the war, driven by weak industrial demand and ample alternative supply.
reuters
+3

The regional picture is equally strained. Asian LNG imports overall fell 8.6% year over year in March to about 20.6 million metric tonnes, the sharpest monthly decline since December 2022. Price-sensitive buyers across South and Southeast Asia have been forced into fuel switching, rationing, or industrial curtailments as spot prices remain far above their comfort zone.
shipuniverse
+1

With President Trump issuing an ultimatum to Tehran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz by April 6, and Ras Laffan potentially offline for four to five months according to Energy Vista founder Leslie Palti-Guzman, the global gas crunch shows few signs of easing soon.
finance.yahoo
+1

FDD tracker: Iran war dominated Trump foreign policy in March

The Foundation for Defense of Democracies released its April edition of the Trump Administration Foreign Policy Tracker on April 1, offering a sweeping assessment of a month in which the war with Iran eclipsed virtually every other item on Washington's foreign policy agenda. The monthly report, which draws on FDD experts across regions and issues, arrives as President Trump has signaled the military campaign could wind down within weeks.
nytimes
+1

Published
3 hours ago
youtube
IDF Drops 15000 Bombs, US Hits 10000 Targets In Iran But IRGC...
iranintl
US hit over 10000 targets in Iran, CENTCOM commander says
jpost
US Air Force strikes 10,000th Iranian target since start of war ...
windward
April 1, 2026: Iran War Maritime Intelligence Daily
kyivindependent.com
A Military Campaign of Historic Scale

Operation Epic Fury, launched on February 28 with joint U.S.-Israeli strikes that killed Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei and destroyed much of Iran's air defense network, reached a milestone on March 25 when CENTCOM Commander Admiral Brad Cooper announced that U.S. forces had struck more than 10,000 military targets in Iran. Israel separately confirmed it had dropped over 15,000 munitions since the war began.
youtube
iranintl
+1

The sustained bombardment has produced dramatic results. By mid-March, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth reported that Iranian missile volume was "down 90%" and one-way attack drones "down 95%" from their peaks on the first day of fighting. Iran entered the war with an estimated 2,500 ballistic missiles; the Jerusalem Post reported that by March 9, launch rates had collapsed from 480 on day one to roughly 40. Yet Iran's selective blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, imposed through a permission-based transit regime routed past Larak Island since approximately March 15, has cut daily vessel crossings by 93% on average, according to maritime intelligence firm Windward.
windward
+2

6 sources
Diplomacy Stalls on Multiple Fronts

Trump's 15-point peace plan, transmitted to Tehran through Pakistan, was rejected by Iran in late March, according to TIME, which cited Iran's state broadcaster Press TV. The proposal called for a one-month ceasefire, a halt to uranium enrichment, dismantlement of key nuclear sites, restrictions on Iran's ballistic missile program, and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. Iran issued a counterproposal demanding war reparations and sovereignty over the strait. Days later, Trump told reporters aboard Air Force One that Iran had agreed to "most of" the 15 points, though no formal deal materialized.
aljazeera
+3

The war's diplomatic gravity pulled attention away from Ukraine. Peace talks between the United States, Russia, and Ukraine — expected to resume in early March — were indefinitely postponed after both Kyiv and Moscow confirmed the pause on March 12. Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov said scheduling had become impossible because "it is unclear when US officials will be able to participate". President Volodymyr Zelensky lamented that talks had been "constantly postponed".
rferl
+1

6 sources
Alliance Strains Deepen

The war has tested relationships with key allies. The Pentagon relocated components of its THAAD missile defense system from South Korea to the Middle East, a move that drew sharp objections from Seoul, which hosts roughly 28,500 U.S. troops as a deterrent against North Korea. South Korean President Lee Jae Myung "expressed opposition" to the withdrawal.
chosun
+1

Meanwhile, most NATO members rejected Washington's call for a coalition to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Germany ruled out involvement, with officials stating "this is not our war," while EU foreign policy chief Kaja Kallas said there was "no appetite" within the bloc to join. France's deputy defense minister said NATO "is not intended to conduct operations in the Strait of Hormuz". Only the United Kingdom signaled tentative interest, though Prime Minister Keir Starmer emphasized any mission would not be a NATO operation.
axios
+2

5 sources

ADNOC CEO calls Iran's Hormuz blockade 'global extortion'

The head of the United Arab Emirates' largest energy company has issued a sharp condemnation of Iran's effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, calling it "global economic extortion" and warning that its consequences are spreading far beyond the Middle East.

Dr. Sultan Ahmed Al Jaber, CEO of Abu Dhabi National Oil Company and UAE Minister of Industry and Advanced Technology, published the rebuke in a LinkedIn post on Tuesday, writing that Iran's actions in the 33-kilometer-wide strait "are not a regional issue" but rather "a threat the world cannot tolerate."
bloomberg
+1

Published
11 hours ago
bloomberg
Iran's Block of Hormuz Oil Flows Is 'Extortion,' Al Jaber Says
linkedin
Dr. Sultan Al Jaber's Post - LinkedIn
argusmedia
LPG Market Outlook: Impact of Iran War, March 2026 | Argus Media
wikipedia
2026 Strait of Hormuz crisis - Wikipedia
citybiz.co
Cascading Economic Damage

Al Jaber outlined a wide range of disruptions already rippling across the global economy. Asian nations have been hit first, he wrote, with shorter working weeks, fuel rationing, reduced flights, and air conditioning cuts. Now the effects are reaching Europe, where food and fuel prices are climbing for consumers and stoking inflation.
linkedin

The strait is a conduit for roughly 20 percent of global traded energy, but Al Jaber stressed the crisis extends well beyond oil. Half of the world's sulphur supply — a chemical essential to pharmaceuticals and fertilizers — passes through Hormuz, along with about 30 percent of global seaborne LPG exports. "This is not about oil supply; it's about everyday affordability for billions of people," he wrote.
argusmedia
+1

Brent crude futures surged roughly 42 percent over the course of March, according to trading data, after Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps effectively shut down shipping through the strait in retaliation for joint U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran that began February 28. As of late March, approximately 2,000 ships were immobilized in the vicinity, according to the International Maritime Organization.
wikipedia
+2

5 sources
Calls for International Action

Al Jaber called on the international community to act in concert. "The world must act — together — to protect the free flow of energy and safeguard economic stability," he wrote.
bloomberg

The appeal comes as the UN Security Council considers a Bahraini draft resolution that would authorize "all necessary means" to secure commercial navigation through the strait. The March 12 Security Council resolution condemning Iran's attacks on Gulf nations passed with 13 votes in favor and abstentions from Russia and China, but neither vetoed it. A more forceful Chapter VII resolution authorizing military action faces stiffer opposition from both Moscow and Beijing.
jns
+2

The UAE has told Washington and allied governments it would join a multinational maritime force to reopen the waterway, according to the Financial Times, and is lobbying other countries to participate. Meanwhile, the UN Secretary-General has established a task force aimed at facilitating humanitarian trade — particularly fertilizers — through the strait as a confidence-building step toward a wider political settlement.
un
+1

Iran doubles down on Hormuz closure as Gulf economies face deepening crisis
On Friday, drones struck Kuwait's Shuwaikh Port, the country's main commercial hub, causing material damage but no casualties, the Kuwait Ports Authority confirmed. Separately, Mubarak Al Kabeer Port — a facility under construction as part of China's Belt and Road Initiative — also sustained damage in the attacks, marking one of the first times a Chinese-affiliated project in the Gulf has come...

Bitcoin briefly tops $69K but whale selling clouds outlook

Bitcoin briefly reclaimed the $69,000 level on Wednesday as hopes of a U.S.-Iran de-escalation lifted risk appetite across global markets, but on-chain data paints a more cautious picture: large holders have turned into persistent net sellers, creating structural selling pressure that institutional buying has struggled to absorb.

Published
11 hours ago
binance
Price forecast for Bitcoin in April 2026 - Binance
mexc
Bitcoin whales ramp up selling as bearish momentum builds
rootdata
CryptoQuant: Bitcoin has fallen below the 365-day moving average ...
noortrends
Bitcoin Jumps as Iran De-Escalation Hopes Spark Broad Crypto Rally
ccn.com
Relief Rally Meets Resistance

The world's largest cryptocurrency twice breached $69,000 during the April 1 session, hitting an intraday high of $69,268 before profit-taking pulled it back below $68,500, according to Bitcoin.com News. The rally followed President Donald Trump's Tuesday evening statement that U.S. military operations against Iran could end within two to three weeks, a signal that triggered a broad risk-on rotation across equities and crypto alike.
noortrends
+2

Yet the bounce came against a backdrop of deteriorating demand fundamentals. CryptoQuant data shows that the Bitcoin Exchange Whale Ratio — measuring the share of the 10 largest deposits to exchanges relative to total inflows — has climbed steadily throughout 2026, reaching 0.79 by late March, up from 0.34 in January. The metric's 30-day moving average continues to trend upward, a pattern that has historically preceded bearish price action. Whales shifted from accumulation to distribution in March, with their 30-day holdings change turning negative, according to CryptoQuant.
binance
+1

5 sources
ETFs and Strategy Inc. Fail to Fill the Gap

Institutional demand has weakened at the same time. CryptoQuant's weekly research noted that U.S. spot Bitcoin ETFs purchased 46,000 BTC in 2025 but net sold 10,600 BTC as of early February 2026, creating a demand gap of roughly 56,000 BTC compared to the prior year. Although March saw a partial recovery with approximately $1.53 billion in ETF inflows that reaccumulated around 38,000 BTC, the final week of March reversed course with $296 million in outflows.
whale-alert
+2

Strategy Inc., formerly MicroStrategy, paused its Bitcoin purchases for the first time in 13 weeks during the period ending March 29, according to an SEC filing. The company holds approximately 762,099 BTC at an average cost of $75,694 — well above the current trading price near $68,000.
finance.yahoo
+1

5 sources
Structural Pressure Persists

The Coinbase Bitcoin Premium Index has remained in negative territory for over 10 consecutive days, sitting at -0.0857% as of late March, reflecting persistent U.S. selling pressure and reduced investor risk appetite. Since mid-October 2025, the premium has been broadly negative, a sharp departure from previous bull markets fueled by U.S. demand.
mexc
+1

Bitcoin remains roughly 46% below its all-time high of $126,198 set on October 6, 2025. Analyst Benjamin Cowen has cautioned that the market cycle may not bottom until late 2026, while trading analyst Ali Martinez has flagged a descending triangle pattern over the past two months that compounds downside risk. Any sustained recovery, analysts say, depends on whether the easing of geopolitical tensions can translate into durable demand — something on-chain metrics have yet to confirm.
fortune
+2

Poland's Tusk warns NATO fractures resemble 'Putin's dream plan'

Polish Prime Minister Donald Tusk warned Thursday that a cascade of geopolitical fractures — from threats of a U.S. exit from NATO to the diversion of weapons away from Ukraine — is playing directly into Moscow's hands, describing the developments as "Putin's dream plan."
yenisafak
+1

"The threat of NATO's break-up, easing sanctions on Russia, a massive energy crisis in Europe, halting aid for Ukraine, and blocking the loan for Kyiv by Orban — it all looks like Putin's dream plan," Tusk said, referring to Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban's obstruction of financial support for Kyiv.
trtworld
+1

Published
12 minutes ago
yenisafak
Polish PM warns Europe's crisis looks like 'Putin's dream plan'
reuters
Trump threatens NATO exit, scaling up tensions with allies
time
Is Trump Pulling the U.S. Out of NATO Over Iran War Fallout? - TIME
youtube
Trump threatens to pull US out of NATO over lack of support from allies
ksat.com
Trump Escalates NATO Threats

The Polish leader's remarks came after President Donald Trump told Reuters he was "absolutely" considering withdrawing the United States from NATO, calling the alliance "a paper tiger." Trump expressed frustration that European allies declined to join offensive operations in the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran, which began on Feb. 28.
reuters
+1
youtube

Secretary of State Marco Rubio reinforced the message, telling Fox News the U.S. may need to "re-examine" whether NATO "is still serving that purpose, or is it now become a one-way street where America is simply in a position to defend Europe." However, Politico reported that for all of Trump's threats, there are few signs an actual withdrawal process has begun.
time
+1

4 sources
Ukraine's Weapons Pipeline Under Pressure

Separately, the Financial Times reported that Trump threatened to halt arms supplies under NATO's Prioritized Ukraine Requirements List (PURL) — the program through which European nations fund purchases of American weapons for Ukraine — unless allies joined a coalition to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte reportedly pushed France, Germany, and the U.K. to issue a March 19 statement expressing willingness to contribute to safe passage through the strait, directly in response to Trump's threat.
iranintl
+2

The PURL program, launched in July 2025, has channeled over $4 billion in ally-funded U.S. arms to Ukraine. But the Pentagon is now weighing whether to redirect roughly $750 million worth of Patriot interceptor missiles originally earmarked for Ukraine to replenish stocks depleted by the Iran conflict, the Washington Post reported. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky said allies were sending "as many anti-ballistic weapons packages as they can" to the Middle East, adding that "unfortunately, they sometimes forget about Ukraine."
euromaidanpress
+3

7 sources
Peace Talks at a Standstill

The diversions come as Ukraine-Russia peace negotiations have stalled. No new round of trilateral talks has been scheduled since a meeting in Geneva in mid-February, with both sides publicly blaming the U.S. focus on Iran. Sources told the BBC that the impasse predated the Middle East escalation, driven by deadlocks over Russian demands for Ukrainian troop withdrawals from Donbas and long-term security guarantees. Warsaw, a key logistical hub for military aid to Ukraine, now finds itself at the center of a widening rift between Washington and its European allies.
bbcrussian.substack
+1

2 sources

Crimson Desert tops 4 million sales in under two weeks

Pearl Abyss announced on April 1 that its open-world action-adventure game Crimson Desert has sold four million copies worldwide, less than two weeks after its March 19 launch across PlayStation 5, Xbox Series X|S, and PC
gematsu
+1
. The milestone caps a turbulent but ultimately triumphant debut for the South Korean studio's most ambitious project to date.

Published
21 hours ago
gematsu
Crimson Desert sales top four million - Gematsu
biz.chosun
Pearl Abyss hits 4 million Crimson Desert sales worldwide
reddit
Crimson Desert developer Pearl Abyss stock price has shot back up ...
windowscentral
From Mixed to Magic — Crimson Desert's Steam review surge
boundingintocomics.com
From Mixed Reviews to Momentum

Crimson Desert moved two million copies within its first 24 hours, but the celebration was tempered by a wave of criticism. Steam reviews sat firmly in "Mixed" territory at launch, with just under 60 percent of early ratings positive, as players flagged clunky controls, limited storage, and technical issues including a failure to support Intel Arc graphics cards. Pearl Abyss's stock plunged roughly 30 percent in the days following release as investors reacted to the lukewarm reception.
gematsu
+5

The studio responded with aggressive patching. Update 1.00.03, released four days after launch, overhauled boss difficulty, reduced stamina costs for blocking, added private storage and new fast-travel points, and fixed graphics card detection problems. The fixes had an immediate effect: daily Steam reviews surged to 98 percent positive in the days that followed, according to SteamDB data cited by Windows Central, pushing the game's overall rating first to "Mostly Positive" and eventually to "Very Positive".
biz.chosun
+4

11 sources
Player Counts and Stock Recovery

Rather than bleeding players after a rough opening, Crimson Desert grew. The game set a new all-time concurrent player peak of 276,261 on Steam on Sunday, March 29, making it the platform's third most-played title behind only Dota 2 and Counter-Strike 2 at the time. Weekday peak concurrent players have held above 200,000.
steamdb
youtube
windowscentral

The turnaround extended to financial markets. After the initial selloff, Pearl Abyss shares rebounded by roughly 23 percent following the three-million-unit announcement on March 24, with the stock climbing close to 30 percent from its post-launch low. The company has signaled plans for post-launch expansions and DLC, and CEO comments reported by VGC indicated the studio is exploring a potential Nintendo Switch 2 port.
videogameschronicle
+2

6 sources
What Comes Next

Crimson Desert reached three million sales by March 24 — just five days after launch — before crossing the four-million mark around March 31. Pearl Abyss has said it will continue iterating based on community feedback, a strategy that has so far converted early skeptics into advocates and kept player engagement climbing.
biz.chosun
+1

2 sources

Houthis claim third strike on Israel, weigh Bab al-Mandab blockade

Yemen's Houthi rebels have opened a new front in the Middle East war, firing missiles at Israel and raising the specter of a blockade at the Bab al-Mandab Strait that could compound the economic damage already inflicted by Iran's restriction of the Strait of Hormuz. Together, the two chokepoints handle nearly a third of the world's seaborne oil supply, and analysts warn that disruptions at both could send crude prices spiraling.

Published
16 hours ago
yahoo
Houthi Threat to Red Sea Shipping Could Reemerge as Iran Conflict ...
axios
Houthis join the Iran war, launch missile at Israel
apnews
Middle East war intensifies with first strikes by Houthis
usnews
Yemen's Houthis Confirm Launching Attack on Israel for First Time in ...
lemonde.fr
A New Front Opens

The Houthis launched their first attack on Israel since the war began on February 28, firing ballistic missiles at what they called "sensitive military sites" in southern Israel on Saturday, March 28. Israel's military said it intercepted the incoming missile, and no casualties were reported. Houthi military spokesperson Yahya Saree said the strikes were carried out "in solidarity with Iran and the resistance fronts" and would continue until hostilities cease.
axios
+3

The group has since escalated. On April 1, the Houthis claimed a "third military operation" targeting sites in southern Israel, conducted in coordination with Hezbollah and Iran. Saree said the attack "successfully achieved its objectives."
wikipedia

5 sources
The Bab al-Mandab Card

More alarming to global markets than the missile strikes on Israel is what the Houthis have not yet done: attack commercial shipping. Houthi deputy information minister Mohammed Mansour told Anadolu Agency that closing the Bab al-Mandab Strait — a 29-kilometer-wide passage between Yemen and Djibouti through which cargo worth $1 trillion passes annually — remains "among our options". "This depends on the Israeli and US escalation, and the American preparations for any ground movement," Mansour said.
aa

The Houthis have demonstrated their capacity to disrupt the strait before. From late 2023 through October 2025, they carried out more than 100 attacks on commercial vessels in the Red Sea, according to the U.S. Maritime Administration, sinking ships and killing seafarers. Bloomberg reported, via European officials, that Iran is encouraging the Houthis to resume attacks on commercial shipping if the U.S. intensifies military action.
yahoo
+1

3 sources
A Stranglehold on Global Energy

The potential closure of Bab al-Mandab would compound the crisis already created by Iran's restrictions on the Strait of Hormuz, which began in early March after U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iranian territory. Major shipping lines including Maersk and Hapag-Lloyd have already suspended transit through Hormuz. Together, the two straits account for roughly 33 percent of the world's oil supply, according to the Center for Strategic and International Studies.
aljazeera
+1
youtube
+1

Brent crude surged nearly 60 percent in March, at one point reaching $119.50 per barrel. Societe Generale forecasts Brent could average $125 per barrel in April, with potential spikes to $150 if the conflict expands further into Red Sea shipping lanes. Suez Canal transits have already dropped 33 percent in the two weeks ending March 22, according to shipping analytics firm Drewry.
tradingkey
+1

Reuters reported that Western powers, having failed to fully secure shipping in the Red Sea during the earlier Houthi campaign, face an even larger challenge at Hormuz, where the danger zone is up to five times bigger. "Our fingers are on the trigger," Houthi spokesperson Saree warned on March 27.
defenceweb
+1

The conflict, which began February 28 with U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran, has already driven Brent crude up roughly 44% in a month. Should the Houthis act, the most consequential move would be blockading the Bab al-Mandeb Strait, the narrow passage between Yemen and the Horn of Africa through which roughly 4.2 million barrels of oil passed daily in the first half of 2025. Iran's effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz has already removed approximately 20% of global oil supplies from the market, according to the Dallas...

As Iran dug in, the Trump administration signaled it is preparing for both diplomacy and escalation. Pakistan has offered to host peace talks in Islamabad, and the U.S. has deployed additional Marines and paratroopers to the region. White House spokesperson Caroline Levitt said that if Iran "does not understand that it has been defeated militarily," President Trump "will deliver a greater blow...

OpenAI secondary market cools as investors flock to Anthropic

Even as OpenAI closed the largest private funding round in history — $122 billion at an $852 billion valuation — signs of cooling demand for the company's shares on secondary markets have emerged, with institutional investors reportedly looking to offload positions while interest in rival Anthropic surges.

The contrast underscores a shifting landscape in the private AI market, where Anthropic's rapid rise in enterprise adoption is redrawing investor allegiances just as both companies prepare for potential initial public offerings later this year.

Published
techcrunch
OpenAI, not yet public, raises $3B from retail investors ... - TechCrunch
bloomberg
OpenAI Valued at $852 Billion After Completing $122 Billion Round
wsj
OpenAI Closes Silicon Valley's Largest-Ever Funding Round - WSJ
forgeglobal
Invest and Sell OpenAI Stock - Forge Global
cnbc.com
A Record Round Amid Quiet Doubts

OpenAI finalized its mammoth fundraising on Monday, with Amazon committing $50 billion and Nvidia and SoftBank each contributing $30 billion, according to Bloomberg. The round also included about $3 billion from individual investors through bank channels and participation from Andreessen Horowitz, TPG, and T. Rowe Price.
techcrunch
+2

Yet behind the headline figures, secondary market data paints a more nuanced picture. On Hiive, a platform for trading private company shares, OpenAI showed zero live orders as of March 31. On Forge Global, the company's estimated share price stood at $728.41 as of April 1 — implying a valuation below the $852 billion set in the primary round. Multiple institutional investors, including hedge funds and venture capital firms, are said to be seeking to sell OpenAI positions, a departure from 2025, when OpenAI shares were among the most sought-after assets in private markets.
forgeglobal
+3

7 sources
Anthropic's Enterprise Surge Draws Capital

The shift in sentiment coincides with Anthropic's ascent. The company closed a $30 billion Series G round in February at a $380 billion valuation, led by GIC and Coatue. Its secondary shares have climbed sharply, roughly doubling from around $150-$175 per share in late 2025 to approximately $300 by January. Business Insider reported that special purpose vehicles marketing Anthropic shares have proliferated as investors scramble for exposure.
augment
+2

Anthropic's momentum stems from its enterprise products. The Wall Street Journal reported in March that OpenAI's leadership had acknowledged Anthropic's gains, prompting a strategic shift toward coding tools and enterprise clients after its broader product strategy — spanning video generation, a web browser, and e-commerce features — lost ground to Anthropic's focused Claude Code and Cowork offerings. The New York Times noted that Anthropic had attracted "thousands of significant corporate clients" in just months. Both companies are reportedly preparing S-1 filings for the second half of 2026.
finance.yahoo
+2

6 sources
The IPO Factor

The divergence may also reflect pre-IPO positioning. As Reuters reported last year, OpenAI has explored an IPO at a valuation of up to $1 trillion, while Anthropic is reportedly planning a late-2026 offering that could raise more than $60 billion. Some investors may be rebalancing portfolios ahead of these listings, rotating toward what they view as the stronger enterprise play. Anthropic has targeted $20 billion to $26 billion in annualized revenue for 2026, while OpenAI — despite generating $13.1 billion in 2025 revenue — is projected to lose roughly $14 billion this year.
reuters
+4

Forbes noted that OpenAI has begun offering private equity firms a guaranteed minimum return of 17.5% to sweeten partnership deals, a move Reuters described as part of an "enterprise turf war" with Anthropic.
finance.yahoo
+1

7 sources

French consumer group sues Ubisoft over The Crew shutdown

French consumer advocacy organization UFC-Que Choisir announced on March 31 that it has filed a lawsuit against Ubisoft over the publisher's decision to shut down its online racing game The Crew, alleging the company engaged in misleading commercial practices and imposed abusive contractual terms on buyers.
gamefragger
+1

The lawsuit, backed by the European consumer movement Stop Killing Games, marks the latest legal challenge facing Ubisoft over the 2024 shutdown of the 2014 title. UFC-Que Choisir argues that players were never clearly informed that their purchase amounted to a temporary license rather than ownership of the game, and that Ubisoft's unilateral revocation of access violated fundamental consumer rights.
wnhub
+1

Published
21 hours ago
notebookcheck
Stop Killing Games launches EU and US watchdog NGOs as Ross ...
gamingonlinux
Stop Destroying Videogames initiative to get a public hearing ...
gamesradar
Ubisoft sued again over The Crew shutdown, this time by French consumer group backed by Stop Killing Games alleging misleading ownership rules
gamefragger
UBISOFT Is Being Sued Over THE CREW Server Shutdown Fiasco
yahoo.com
A Growing Legal Storm

Ubisoft delisted The Crew from digital storefronts in December 2023 and shut down the game's servers on March 31, 2024, rendering it permanently unplayable. The company then began stripping users' licenses, preventing them from even installing the game. The decision proved to be a public relations disaster, spurring the creation of the Stop Killing Games campaign by YouTuber Ross Scott and eventually generating over 1.3 million validated signatures on a European Citizens' Initiative petition.
pcgamer
+4

UFC-Que Choisir is seeking court-ordered compensation for affected players and a reassessment of how Ubisoft sells always-online games. A separate class-action lawsuit filed by two California gamers in late 2024 is also reportedly ongoing, though its current status remains unclear.
gamesradar
+3

9 sources
EU Action on the Horizon

The lawsuit arrives at a pivotal moment for the broader game preservation movement. Moritz Katzner, Stop Killing Games' general director, told Reuters that the European Parliament is expected to hold a hearing on the matter on April 16. The European Commission, which received the validated petition in January, has until July 27, 2026, to issue a formal response on whether it will propose legislation requiring publishers to keep sold games in a functional state.
gamingonlinux
+3

Ross Scott, the campaign's founder, said in a February update that the movement has "majority support" within the European Parliament and that the issue "is in a good position to get passed". Stop Killing Games has also launched non-governmental organizations in both the EU and the United States to handle lobbying and enable consumers to report publishers who disable purchased games.
notebookcheck
+1
youtube

In a 2025 legal filing related to the California lawsuit, Ubisoft argued that players had already enjoyed The Crew for a decade "and cannot complain now [...] simply because Ubisoft did not then create an offline version of the discontinued video game".
gamesradar

8 sources

Chinese stocks, bonds outperform global peers amid Iran war

More than a month into the US-Israeli military campaign against Iran, China is absorbing the economic shock of disrupted oil supplies and soaring energy costs — yet a growing number of analysts argue the world's second-largest economy may be better positioned than most to weather the storm, and could even emerge from the crisis stronger.

Published
3 hours ago
businessinsider
The Iran war could end up helping China's economy - Business Insider
businessinsider
Goldman says that China will emerge from the Iran war with a leg up on the US
nytimes
For China, Billions of Dollars Are at Risk From a Widening War
+40 sources
cnbc.com
Short-Term Pain, Strategic Buffers

The conflict, which began with coordinated US-Israeli strikes on February 28, has effectively shut down shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, a chokepoint that normally carries roughly half of China's crude oil imports. Iran alone accounted for about 13 percent of China's imported crude before the war. Oil prices have surged roughly 50 percent above prewar levels, with Brent crude recently trading above $100 a barrel.
businessinsider
+3

But Beijing spent much of the past year preparing. Chinese oil imports rose 15.8 percent in January and February compared to the year prior, building reserves that analysts at the Peterson Institute for International Economics estimate now stand at 1.3 billion to 1.4 billion barrels — roughly four months of imports. Discounted Russian crude, which comprised over a fifth of China's total imported crude by volume in early 2026, has provided an additional cushion. Goldman Sachs estimated that China will see a smaller drag on GDP from the oil shock than the United States.
nytimes
+3

7 sources
Bonds, Renewables, and the Petroyuan

Chinese government bonds have become a rare bright spot for global investors. While US and UK 10-year yields have jumped by 0.38 to 0.7 percentage points since the war began, Chinese yields have edged down, with the 10-year holding steady near 1.8 percent, according to Bloomberg. The Financial Times attributed this resilience to China's low inflation, capital controls, and domestic investor demand.
bloomingbit
+1

Markets are also pricing in a renewable energy windfall. The CSI Green Electricity Index has risen about 10 percent since the war began, even as the CSI 300 fell roughly 4 percent. Battery maker Contemporary Amperex Technology has jumped 27 percent. "For China, Hormuz disruptions are certainly painful. Yet they also vindicate Beijing's bet on electrification," wrote Agathe Demarais, a senior policy fellow at the European Council on Foreign Relations.
businessinsider

3 sources
Diplomatic Setback in the Gulf

The war has, however, dealt a blow to China's decade-long diplomatic strategy in the Middle East. The Iran-Saudi Arabia rapprochement that Beijing brokered in March 2023 — once hailed as proof of China's growing role as an international mediator — has effectively collapsed under the weight of Iranian missile and drone strikes on Saudi targets. On March 27, Iran struck Kuwait's Mubarak Al-Kabeer Port, a flagship Belt and Road Initiative project, in what analysts called the first direct targeting of a Chinese-linked strategic asset.
youtube
wikipedia
+2

With President Donald Trump announcing on Tuesday that US forces could withdraw from Iran within two to three weeks, the conflict's trajectory remains uncertain. But as one analyst at Bank of America put it, China's diversified energy mix leaves its economy relatively well set for "extreme scenarios".
chosun
+2

7 sources
Discover more
China's energy buffers outpace Asian neighbors amid Hormuz closure
The crisis is forcing broader strategic recalculations across East Asia. Fortune reported that the Hormuz closure is pushing the region simultaneously backward — toward coal — and forward, toward nuclear energy and accelerated EV adoption. Japan and South Korea have begun deepening bilateral energy cooperation, according to The Diplomat, as individual self-sufficiency proves insufficient. Yet...
Goldman Sachs says China better shielded from oil shock than U.S.
Despite China's relative resilience, Goldman warned that the country is not immune to second-order effects. The bank flagged global stagflation risks, noting that rising correlations across asset classes are reducing the effectiveness of traditional portfolio diversification. A stronger US dollar, driven by safe-haven flows, could also weigh on Chinese equities and emerging markets more...
China's factory activity rebounds as Iran war roils Asian economies
India may be among the most vulnerable economies in the Asia-Pacific. An EY report published Sunday projected that if the conflict persists through fiscal year 2027, India's real GDP growth could erode by around one percentage point while retail inflation could rise by approximately 1.5 percentage points from baseline estimates. The country imports about 90% of its crude oil and relies on the...
China's Belt and Road projects in Iran face growing instability amid US-Israel war
Beyond energy, the war has eroded China's geopolitical leverage. According to the Daily Mirror report, last June's Operation Midnight Hammer and the ongoing Epic Fury campaign have "devastated the nuclear infrastructure of Iran and its conventional long-range missiles and drone capabilities," leaving Iran unable to function as a strategic tool for Beijing. Chinese investments in railways,...

Tesla braces for soft deliveries as Chinese EV makers post record Q1

Tesla is expected to report a sequential decline in first-quarter vehicle deliveries when it releases results on Thursday, even as Chinese electric vehicle competitors announced robust March figures that sent their shares higher in pre-market trading on Wednesday.

Published
22 hours ago
electrek
Tesla (TSLA) publishes Q1 2026 delivery consensus - Electrek
teslahubs
Tesla Releases Q1 2026 Delivery Estimates From Analysts
finance.yahoo
Tesla Q1 deliveries expected to rebound slightly in challenging EV ...
teslaacessories
Tesla's 2026 Stock Rout: Delivery Warnings and the Trillion-Dollar ...
cnbc.com
Analysts Brace for a Dip

The company's own compilation of 23 Wall Street analyst estimates projects 365,645 deliveries for Q1 2026, according to Tesla's investor relations page — a roughly 12.5% drop from the 418,227 vehicles delivered in Q4 2025, though still an approximately 8.6% increase from the 336,681 units delivered in Q1 2025. Bloomberg's consensus sits slightly lower at 364,645, while some analysts such as UBS project figures as low as 345,000.
electrek
+4

The expected sequential decline reflects familiar seasonal patterns in the auto industry, but analysts say deeper headwinds are also at play. The expiration of the U.S. federal EV tax credit on September 30, 2025, eliminated up to $7,500 in incentives for new EV buyers, removing a key purchase driver. Intensifying competition in Europe and China has further eroded Tesla's market position, while ongoing brand damage linked to CEO Elon Musk's political activities continues to weigh on demand. An Associated Press report last year noted that boycotts tied to Musk's alignment with far-right politics had a "more profound and enduring" impact on sales than many investors anticipated.
argusmedia
+2

Tesla has now recorded two consecutive years of declining annual deliveries, falling from a peak of 1.81 million in 2023 to 1.64 million in 2025. The full-year 2026 analyst consensus stands at just 1.69 million vehicles, a 3.3% increase.
tesla
+2

8 sources
Chinese Rivals Surge

In contrast, NIO, XPeng, and Li Auto all reported strong March delivery rebounds on Tuesday. NIO delivered 35,486 vehicles in March, up 136% year-over-year, bringing its Q1 total to 83,465 units — a 98.3% surge from a year ago and above its own guidance range of 80,000 to 83,000. The results spanned NIO's three brands: 22,490 premium NIO vehicles, 6,877 from the family-oriented ONVO line, and 6,119 from the compact FIREFLY brand.
247wallst
+1

XPeng delivered 27,415 vehicles in March, an 80% sequential increase, though Q1 deliveries of 62,682 represented a decline of more than 30% year-over-year. Li Auto reported 41,053 March deliveries, lifting its Q1 total to 95,142 units. The company said production bottlenecks for its Li i6 model had been resolved, with the vehicle surpassing 24,000 monthly deliveries in March.
stocktitan
+3

6 sources
What Comes Next

Tesla is scheduled to release its official Q1 production and delivery figures on Thursday, April 2. How the numbers compare to the subdued consensus will set the tone heading into the company's earnings report on April 22. On prediction market Polymarket, the leading outcome for Q1 deliveries remains "under 350,000" with a 63.5% implied probability, suggesting some investors expect Tesla to miss even the lowered bar Wall Street has set.
teslahubs
+2

Police, FIFA probe anti-Muslim chants at Spain-Egypt friendly

Anti-Muslim chants by Spanish fans during a pre-World Cup friendly between Spain and Egypt on Tuesday marred what was meant to be a routine warm-up match, prompting investigations by local police and a review by FIFA that could result in sanctions against the Spanish national team.

Published
15 hours ago
espn
Spain's Lamine Yamal condemns anti-Muslim chants; police ... - ESPN
surinenglish
Spain–Egypt friendly in Barcelona marred by alleged Islamophobic ...
as
No consequences for the World Cup - AS USA - Diario AS
dw
Spain: Anti-Muslim chants mar Egypt soccer friendly
aljazeera.com
"Disrespectful and Intolerable"

The match, held at RCDE Stadium in Barcelona, ended in a 0-0 draw but was overshadowed by repeated Islamophobic chanting from sections of the crowd. Fans sang "Musulmán el que no bote" — roughly, "If you're not jumping, you're a Muslim" — during the first half, while the Egyptian national anthem was met with loud whistling before kickoff. Stadium officials displayed anti-discrimination messages on screens at halftime and again early in the second half, but the referee did not activate FIFA's anti-racism protocol.
as
+2

Spain star Lamine Yamal, an 18-year-old practicing Muslim whose father is Moroccan, posted a pointed response on Instagram. "I am Muslim, alhamdulillah," he wrote. "I know it was against the opposition and nothing personal, but as a Muslim it is still disrespectful and completely intolerable. To use religion as a way of mocking others inside a stadium is ignorant and racist".
espn

Head coach Luis de la Fuente said he felt "total and absolute disgust" toward "any form of xenophobia, racism, and disrespect".
dw
+1

5 sources
Investigations and Potential FIFA Sanctions

Catalonia's regional police force, the Mossos d'Esquadra, announced on Wednesday that it is investigating "Islamophobic and xenophobic chants" from the match in coordination with the Prosecutor's Office for Hate Crimes and Discrimination. Officers are analyzing footage and social media content to identify those responsible.
surinenglish
+1

A source told ESPN that FIFA will also analyze reports from the referee, the match inspector, and the on-site security team, with Spain facing potential sanctions ahead of the 2026 World Cup. The Egyptian Football Association called the chanting a "reprehensible racist act" and said it is working with FIFA officials to prevent future incidents. FIFA itself stated that "what happened is completely unacceptable" and reaffirmed its commitment to combating racism.
espn

3 sources
Broader Context

The incident arrives amid heightened scrutiny of racism in football. Earlier this year, Real Madrid's Vinícius Júnior alleged racist abuse from Benfica's Gianluca Prestianni during a Champions League match, leading to a UEFA investigation and provisional suspension. Berni Álvarez, Catalonia's sports minister, criticized organizers for a delayed response, saying the match "should have been stopped" after the first chant. With the World Cup approaching this summer, Spain now faces the unwelcome possibility of disciplinary action at a moment when the sport's governing bodies are under pressure to show that anti-racism protocols are more than words on paper.
aljazeera
+2

Amnesty called on FIFA and host governments to guarantee freedom of expression and peaceful assembly at and around World Cup venues, exclude ICE from enforcement operations near stadiums, lift travel bans on fans from participating nations, and publish transparent human rights strategies for all host cities. Italy miss third straight World Cup as 48-team field is set

Four-time champions Italy will miss a third consecutive FIFA World Cup after a devastating penalty shootout loss to Bosnia-Herzegovina on Tuesday, a result that filled the last remaining European berths and completed the 48-team lineup for the expanded tournament set to kick off on June 11 across the United States, Canada, and Mexico.

Published
Apr 1, 2026
skysports
World Cup 2026: Who has qualified? Full list of teams for USA ...
espn
Bosnia-Herz 1-1 Italy (Mar 31, 2026) Final Score - ESPN
espn
Bosnia-Herz 1-1 Italy (Mar 31, 2026) Game Analysis - ESPN
+40 sources
rte.ie
Italy's Collapse in Zenica

Italy's campaign ended in agony at Bilino Polje Stadium in Zenica, where a first-half red card to defender Alessandro Bastoni proved fatal. Moise Kean had given the Azzurri an early lead in the 15th minute, but Haris Tabakovic equalized for Bosnia in the 79th, and the match finished 1-1 after extra time. In the shootout, Italian substitutes Pio Esposito and Bryan Cristante both failed from the spot, and Esmir Bajraktarevic converted the winning penalty to seal a 4-1 shootout victory for Bosnia.
espn
+1

The defeat extends Italy's absence from football's biggest stage to 12 years. The Azzurri have not appeared at a World Cup since Brazil 2014, having failed to qualify for both the 2018 and 2022 editions. Bosnia, meanwhile, will make their second World Cup appearance when they take the field in Group B alongside Canada, Qatar, and Switzerland.
washingtonpost
+2

All 48 Spots Now Filled

Tuesday's results also settled the final intercontinental playoff places. Iraq secured their first World Cup berth since 1986 by defeating Bolivia 2-1 in Monterrey, Mexico, ending a 40-year wait. DR Congo also qualified by seeing off Jamaica, returning to the tournament for the first time in over five decades.
gulfnews
+1

The six final qualifiers — Sweden, Turkey, Bosnia-Herzegovina, and the Czech Republic through UEFA playoffs, plus Iraq and DR Congo via the intercontinental route — round out a full field of 48 nations.
skysports

The tournament will feature 104 matches across 16 stadiums over 39 days, opening on June 11 when Mexico face South Africa at Estadio Azteca in Mexico City. Among the most anticipated group-stage matchups is Group I, where France will meet Senegal at MetLife Stadium on June 14 before facing Norway and Iraq in a group that brings together Kylian Mbappé and Erling Haaland.

The final is scheduled for July 19 at MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey. Questions linger over Iran's participation in Group G amid the ongoing conflict in the Persian Gulf, with Iran's sports minister having stated that the national team would not take part, though FIFA has said the team remains welcome.

The 2026 FIFA World Cup field is set. Following a night of drama on March 31 that saw Italy eliminated for a third consecutive tournament and Iraq return to the world stage for the first time in 40 years, all 48 teams are now confirmed for the expanded edition of the competition, which will run from June 11 to July 19 across 16 host cities in the United States, Canada, and Mexico.

The final at MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey, will cap a 39-day, 104-match tournament — the largest World Cup ever staged.

Six Teams Clinch Final Spots

Tuesday's playoffs delivered high stakes across six simultaneous matches. Iraq secured the 48th and final berth with a 2-1 victory over Bolivia at Estadio BBVA in Monterrey, ending a World Cup absence stretching back to Mexico 1986. Iraq will slot into Group I alongside France, Senegal, and Norway, with a group-stage meeting against France scheduled for June 22 at Lincoln Financial Field in Philadelphia.
sportingnews
+3

DR Congo also punched their ticket through the intercontinental playoffs, beating Jamaica 1-0 in extra time on a goal from former Manchester United defender Axel Tuanzebe to join Group K with Portugal, Colombia, and Uzbekistan.
aljazeera
+1

In the European playoffs, Türkiye qualified for the first time since their celebrated 2002 semi-final run, winning 1-0 at Kosovo on a Kerem Aktürkoglu goal, according to Reuters. They will face co-hosts the United States, Paraguay, and Australia in Group D. Sweden edged Poland 3-2 thanks to an 88th-minute winner from Viktor Gyökeres to claim a spot in Group F. The Czech Republic beat Denmark on penalties to enter Group A, while Bosnia and Herzegovina stunned four-time champions Italy 4-1 in a shootout in Zenica, as reported by the BBC.
sports.yahoo

youtube

Key Matchups and Schedule

The tournament opens June 11 with host nation Mexico facing South Africa at Estadio Azteca in Mexico City. The United States begins its campaign the following day against Paraguay at SoFi Stadium in Los Angeles, with further Group D matches against Australia and Türkiye to follow.
roadtrips

Group C features Brazil paired with Morocco, Haiti, and Scotland, while defending champions Argentina will face Algeria, Austria, and Jordan in Group J. England's path through Group L includes Croatia, Ghana, and Panama, with their final group match set for MetLife Stadium on June 27.
